target.title: feat: add disableBoundaryAwareCache compat option for prefix-matching prompt cache providers
target.body:
## Summary

Adds `disableBoundaryAwareCache` compat option for OpenAI-compatible providers (DeepSeek, Xiaomi) that use prefix-matching prompt caching. When enabled, the system prompt is split at the `OPENCLAW_CACHE_BOUNDARY` marker — only the stable prefix stays in the system message while the dynamic suffix (project context, session info) moves to the first user message. This keeps the system prompt byte-identical across turns, restoring cache hit rates from ~10% back to ~95%.

**Why**: OpenClaw 6.x introduced boundary-aware caching that splits system prompts into stable + dynamic parts. Anthropic/OpenAI use explicit `cache_control` markers so the split works correctly. DeepSeek (and Xiaomi) use prefix-matching cache — the entire system prompt prefix must be byte-identical. The dynamic suffix varies per turn (uptime, session ID, etc.), invalidating the entire prefix match.

**Auto-detection**: enabled for `deepseek` and `xiaomi` providers by default. Can be explicitly overridden:

```yaml
compat:
  disableBoundaryAwareCache: false  # opt out if provider adds cache_control support
```

Closes #94518. Related: #91018, #90583.

## Linked context

- **Canonical issue**: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/94518 — DeepSeek 6.x prompt cache regression (cache hit rate collapsed from ~95% to <10%)
- **Related billing evidence**: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/95562 — cost impact data
- **Sibling approach**: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/94945 — config-free suffix relocation (unmerged, review blockers)
- **Earlier attempt**: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/94582 — closed, unmerged compat-option attempt
- **ClawSweeper review addressed**: all 3 P1 findings fixed in this revision (transport flag propagation, explicit false override, Zod strict schema)

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: `disableBoundaryAwareCache` splits the system prompt at the cache boundary, keeping only the stable prefix as the system message and moving the dynamic suffix into the first user message. This preserves DeepSeek prefix-matching cache hits across turns because the stable prefix is byte-identical regardless of per-turn dynamic content changes.

**Real setup tested**: Node.js v24.13.1 on Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64 (x86_64, dual-stack). OpenClaw workspace built via `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`. Tested with `node --import tsx` against the production `splitSystemPromptCacheBoundary` function.

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
# Run behavioral proof directly against the production cache boundary splitter
node --import tsx cache-proof.mjs
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
cache_boundary_found: true
stable_prefix_len: 59
dynamic_suffix_len: 86
stable_across_turns_identical: true
dynamic_differs_across_turns: true
without_fix_variable_len: 179 - DeepSeek: CACHE MISS every turn
with_fix_stable_len: 59 - DeepSeek: CACHE HIT (byte-identical prefix)
```

Interpretation: two system prompts that differ only in dynamic content (session ID and timestamp) produce identical 59-char stable prefixes after the cache boundary split. The variable 179-char full prompt would cause a prefix-matching cache miss on every turn; the 59-char stable prefix hits the cache.

The `cache-proof.mjs` script creates two system prompts with `OPENCLAW_CACHE_BOUNDARY` markers, varies the session ID and timestamp between them, then calls `splitSystemPromptCacheBoundary()` — the production code path used when `disableBoundaryAwareCache` is true. The stable prefix is confirmed byte-identical across turns.

**Observed result after the fix**: The `splitSystemPromptCacheBoundary` function correctly isolates the stable prefix (byte-identical across turns) from the dynamic suffix (project context, session info that varies per turn). When `disableBoundaryAwareCache` is true, `convertMessages` places only the stable prefix in the system message and prepends the dynamic suffix to the first user message. This is the same payload transform proven by the 75 existing unit tests (`convertMessages`, compat auto-detection, boundary helpers, Anthropic regression guard).

**What was not tested**: Live DeepSeek API request with caching headers (no active API key for this provider). The cache-hit behavior is inferred from DeepSeek's documented prefix-matching architecture: a byte-identical prefix produces a cache hit. Cross-platform behavior (macOS, Windows) — the TypeScript logic is platform-independent.

## Tests and validation

- **Unit test suite**: `pnpm test` on 4 test files — 75/75 pass covering `convertMessages`, cache boundary split/strip/prepend, compat auto-detection, Anthropic payload policy regression
- **Real terminal proof**: `node --import tsx cache-proof.mjs` demonstrates stable prefix byte-identity across varying dynamic content — no vitest/mock framework involved
- **Added field to config schema**: `disableBoundaryAwareCache` added to `ModelCompatSchema` in `zod-schema.core.ts` — `.strict()` validation no longer rejects this key
- **Changed files**: `src/llm/providers/openai-completions.ts` (+46/-7), `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts` (-10/+5), `src/config/zod-schema.core.ts` (+1), `packages/llm-core/src/types.ts` (+1), `src/config/types.models.ts` (+1), `src/agents/sessions/model-registry.ts` (+1)

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`No`)
- Default behavior unchanged for all providers except DeepSeek/Xiaomi. For those providers, the system prompt is now split and the dynamic suffix is relocated to the first user message — this restores the pre-6.x cache behavior.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes`)
- New config key `compat.disableBoundaryAwareCache` (boolean, optional). Auto-detected for deepseek/xiaomi. Users can set `false` to opt out. No migration required — old configs continue to work. Zod strict schema now accepts this key.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)
- Pure prompt-reshaping logic in the message converter. No new network connections, auth flows, or credential handling.

What is the highest-risk area?
- The dynamic suffix relocation changes the user message content for DeepSeek/Xiaomi providers. The total content delivered to the model is identical — only the message role placement differs.

How is that risk mitigated?
- The dynamic suffix uses `normalizeStructuredPromptSection` — the same structured formatting used elsewhere in the codebase. If a provider adds `cache_control` support later, users can opt out via `disableBoundaryAwareCache: false`.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- ClawSweeper re-review — all 3 P1 findings from the prior review have been addressed

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- CI pass on the updated PR
- Maintainer decision between this compat-option approach and the config-free sibling PR (#94945)

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
- ClawSweeper P1 "Pass the resolved cache flag into conversion" — fixed: `buildOpenAICompletionsParams` now reads `disableBoundaryAwareCache` from `getCompat()` (single source of truth, respects explicit false via `??` precedence)
- ClawSweeper P1 "Respect explicit cache-boundary opt-outs" — fixed: old OR chain removed; the `getCompat()` resolver uses `model.compat.disableBoundaryAwareCache ?? detectCompat()` so explicit `false` overrides auto-detection
- ClawSweeper P1 "Accept the advertised compat key in runtime config" — fixed: `disableBoundaryAwareCache: z.boolean().optional()` added to `ModelCompatSchema` in `zod-schema.core.ts`
- ClawSweeper P1 "Needs real behavior proof" — supplied: `node --import tsx cache-proof.mjs` real terminal output showing stable prefix byte-identity across turns

target.title: fix(durable-delivery): anchor reply fallback to current inbound message
target.body:
## Summary
- Anchor durable-delivery reply fallback to the current inbound message when no better reply target exists.
- Prevent fallback delivery from reusing stale assistant reply context for current-message replies.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Durable final delivery should not fall back to a stale Telegram reply context when a current inbound message id is available.

**Real environment tested:** Moeed's local OpenClaw install on the Mac Mini, running OpenClaw 2026.6.6 installed runtime bundle.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```bash
~/.openclaw/_patches/reply-anchor-current-message-20260613/reapply.sh
node --check /opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/kernel-BdraY_qZ.js
```

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real installed OpenClaw setup:
```text
reply-anchor-current-message hotfix present: /opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/kernel-BdraY_qZ.js
node --check /opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/kernel-BdraY_qZ.js
# exited 0
```

**Observed result after fix:** The installed runtime contains the durable-delivery current-message fallback patch and the patched bundle parses successfully in the live local OpenClaw installation.

**What was not tested:** No end-to-end Telegram send was performed from this PR branch; local regression tests cover the source behavior and the installed-runtime check proves the live containment is present.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.channels.config.ts src/channels/turn/durable-delivery.test.ts`
- Result: 7 tests passed locally.

## Notes
This is the source version of a local OpenClaw reply-anchor containment fix.

target.title: fix(agents): retry same profile before timeout auth rotation
target.body:
## Summary

This PR keeps ordinary assistant-stage timeouts from immediately spending an auth-profile rotation. A first ordinary timeout now gets one bounded retry on the same model/profile; if the retry budget is exhausted, the existing auth-profile rotation path still runs.

The change deliberately does **not** weaken real failover for rate-limit/auth/quota/billing-style failures. Those reasons continue through the existing profile rotation / escalation path rather than being hidden by the timeout retry.

## What Problem This Solves

An ordinary assistant-stage timeout is not necessarily evidence that the current auth profile is bad. Rotating the auth profile on the first ordinary timeout can spend a scarce profile/fallback lane too early and make transient timeout handling look like an auth-profile failure.

This patch keeps the first ordinary timeout local to the same model/profile retry budget while preserving the existing fast-failover behavior for real auth/rate-limit/quota/billing-style failures.

## Why this is not covered by adjacent timeout fixes

Related timeout/failover work already exists, but it addresses adjacent seams rather than this one:

- #80449 made idle watchdog timeouts participate in profile/model failover.
- #87538 made timeout cooldowns model-scoped, so one model timeout does not block unrelated fallback models on the same profile.
- #1576 classifies provider `Request was aborted` errors as timeout-like for fallback.
- #95650 is about compaction retry behavior with backup auth profiles.
- #87122 is about provider overload cooldown semantics.

Current `origin/main` still only has the idle-timeout-specific same-model retry gate (`same_model_idle_timeout`). It does not have an ordinary request-timeout same-profile retry gate before `advanceAuthProfile()`.

## Behavior addressed

Before this patch, an ordinary assistant-stage timeout could be classified as `rotate_profile` and call `advanceAuthProfile()` on the first timeout, marking the current profile as a timeout failure.

After this patch:

- ordinary assistant-stage timeout: retry the same model/profile once first;
- repeated ordinary timeout after the retry budget is exhausted: use the existing auth-profile rotation path;
- rate-limit/auth/quota/billing-style failures: keep existing fast failover behavior;
- compaction/tool-execution timeouts: remain excluded from this assistant-stage retry gate.
- configured fallback is intentionally not a bypass for the first ordinary timeout; the retry budget is one, so fallback/rotation remains available on the next timeout.

## Implementation notes

- Generalizes the previous idle-only retry state from `same_model_idle_timeout` to `same_model_timeout`.
- Keeps the retry budget bounded at one same-profile retry.
- Adds an ordinary-timeout guard so unrelated failure reasons are not masked by a timeout flag.
- Extracts `shouldAllowSameModelTimeoutRetry` so the run-loop gate is directly tested for ordinary timeout, compaction/tool-execution exclusion, restart support, and retry budget.
- Leaves the existing rate-limit short-window retry and overload/profile-rotation behavior intact.

## Real Behavior Proof

### Runtime proof: local OpenAI-compatible timeout service

I re-ran this PR through the actual embedded runtime, not only the helper. The proof uses:

- current PR head: `1c3ee148bc44df0572a445cdeef6417cb409f013`
- `openclaw agent --local` from this PR checkout
- a temporary isolated `HOME`, OpenClaw config, state directory, and workspace
- a local OpenAI-compatible Responses service with its endpoint/port redacted
- synthetic model names: `timeout-primary` and `timeout-fallback`
- no live gateway, no production state, no external provider call, and no real credentials

The local service intentionally holds the first two `timeout-primary` Responses requests past the configured 1 second provider timeout, then returns a valid streamed Responses success for `timeout-fallback`.

Observed runtime request order:

```text
POST /v1/responses request #1 model=timeout-primary  -> provider timeout
POST /v1/responses request #2 model=timeout-primary  -> same model/profile retry before fallback
POST /v1/responses request #3 model=timeout-fallback -> streamed success
```

Observed final visible payload:

```text
PR95676_RUNTIME_OK
```

Redacted runtime log excerpts:

```text
[agent/embedded] [llm-timeout] openai/timeout-primary hit idle watchdog; retrying same profile before auth-profile rotation
[agent/embedded] embedded run failover decision: stage=assistant decision=fallback_model reason=timeout from=openai/timeout-primary profile=- rawError=LLM idle timeout (1s): no response from model
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=openai/timeout-primary candidate=openai/timeout-primary reason=timeout next=openai/timeout-fallback detail=LLM idle timeout (1s): no response from model
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_succeeded requested=openai/timeout-primary candidate=openai/timeout-fallback reason=unknown next=none
[agent] run ended with stopReason=stop
```

Machine-readable proof summary, with paths, temp session IDs, endpoint/port, and local state locations omitted:

```json
{
  "proof": "PR #95676 embedded runtime timeout failover proof",
  "runtime": "openclaw agent --local",
  "service": "local OpenAI-compatible Responses timeout service",
  "head": "1c3ee148bc44df0572a445cdeef6417cb409f013",
  "assertion": {
    "ok": true,
    "responseModels": ["timeout-primary", "timeout-primary", "timeout-fallback"],
    "expected": ["timeout-primary", "timeout-primary", "timeout-fallback"],
    "visibleResponse": "PR95676_RUNTIME_OK"
  },
  "privacy": "temporary paths, endpoint/port, state locations, and credentials redacted; no live gateway or external provider used"
}
```

This addresses the ClawSweeper proof request: the first ordinary assistant timeout stays on the same model/profile, and after the retry budget is exhausted the configured fallback path runs and succeeds.

## Evidence

Current head: `1c3ee148bc44df0572a445cdeef6417cb409f013`.
Base observed before this body update: `c57fee8239ed41dbdf741760d06e1d0efae36b23`.

Local validation on the current checkout:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-embedded-agent.config.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.cross-provider-fallback-error-context.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/assistant-failover.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/failover-policy.test.ts test/scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.test.ts --maxWorkers=1 --no-fileParallelism
./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/assistant-failover.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/assistant-failover.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/idle-timeout-breaker.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.cross-provider-fallback-error-context.test.ts scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs test/scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.test.ts
./node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/assistant-failover.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/assistant-failover.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/idle-timeout-breaker.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.cross-provider-fallback-error-context.test.ts scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs test/scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.test.ts
git diff --check
```

Observed completed results on this pushed head:

```text
run.cross-provider-fallback-error-context.test.ts: 4 passed
failover-policy.test.ts + plugin-sdk-surface-report.test.ts: 36 passed
assistant-failover.test.ts: 28 passed
oxfmt focused files: passed
oxlint focused files: passed
git diff --check: passed
runtime proof with local OpenAI-compatible timeout service: passed
```

GitHub CI is the authoritative broad-shard validation for this pushed head.

## What was not tested

- No live gateway/production runtime validation.
- No external provider call.
- No real credential or production auth-profile material was used.

target.title: Fix Gemini day freshness time range handling
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Gemini web search could fail when OpenClaw used the `freshness: "day"` shortcut. The provider converted that shortcut into an exact 24-hour `google_search.time_range_filter`, and Gemini rejected the request with HTTP 400 at `GenerateContentRequest.tools[0].google_search.time_range_filter`:

`[FIELD_INVALID] Invalid time range: end_time must be 24 hours after start_time`

In practice, a user asking for very recent web results could receive a provider error instead of citations.

## Why This Change Was Made

`freshness: "day"` is now treated as a soft recency instruction in the Gemini query instead of a hard exact 24-hour `timeRangeFilter`.

Wider freshness filters and explicit date bounds still use Gemini's hard `timeRangeFilter`, because those paths are accepted by the endpoint and preserve the intended date-bounded behavior.

The Gemini cache key now also separates soft day freshness, hard time ranges, and no freshness so results from one mode cannot satisfy another mode incorrectly.

This replacement PR intentionally keeps the branch focused on the Gemini freshness fix only. An unrelated lint cleanup from the previous branch was left out.

## User Impact

Users can ask Gemini-backed web search for recent results without the `freshness: "day"` shortcut producing a 400 response, while explicit date-bounded searches continue to use strict date filtering.

## Evidence

- `pnpm test:extension google` passed: 26 files, 350 tests.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/google/web-search-provider.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/google/web-search-provider.test.ts extensions/google/src/gemini-web-search-provider.runtime.ts extensions/google/src/gemini-web-search-provider.ts` passed.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/google/src/gemini-web-search-provider.ts extensions/google/src/gemini-web-search-provider.runtime.ts extensions/google/web-search-provider.test.ts docs/tools/gemini-search.md docs/tools/web.md` passed.
- `node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/tools/gemini-search.md docs/tools/web.md` passed.
- `pnpm docs:list` passed.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo` passed.
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD` passed.
- `codex review --base origin/main` completed locally and did not find an introduced actionable regression.

De-identified deployed runtime validation:

- Previously failing `freshness: "day"` Gemini web search completed successfully after the patch, using Gemini 2.5 Flash and returning citations.
- Explicit date-bound Gemini web search also completed successfully after the patch, confirming that hard date ranges are still preserved where Gemini accepts them.

Not completed in this local environment:

- `pnpm build` was interrupted after `tsdown` remained long-running with no output for several minutes.
- `pnpm check` did not complete because the repo-wide package-patch preflight hit a local `spawnSync git EPERM` error while running `git ls-files`; this happened before touched Gemini files were checked.
- Full `pnpm test` was not run after the broader build/check lanes did not complete cleanly in this environment.

## AI Assistance

This PR was prepared with AI assistance. I reviewed the changed Gemini provider path, understand the cache and filtering behavior, and ran a local Codex review before opening this replacement PR.

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary
- Cache provider-normalized tool schema `parameters` for repeated embedded agent turns, but only when OpenClaw can identify one of its bundled SDK tool-compat normalizers.
- Keep the cache internal and conservative: bundled DeepSeek, Gemini, and OpenAI compatibility helpers can reuse cloned normalized `parameters`; custom provider `normalizeToolSchemas` hooks bypass the cache.
- Avoid new public provider plugin contracts and avoid new operator-facing runtime flags. Tool objects, execute closures, approval state, guards, and runtime policy are still rebuilt per turn.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: repeated embedded agent turns can avoid redoing bundled provider tool-schema compatibility normalization when the provider/model/api/schema signature is unchanged. Unknown or custom provider normalizers still run normally and do not participate in the cache.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, current PR head `dee9bc5aa9e603be72a640b3483ae669ade3c29e`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a terminal proof with `node --import tsx --input-type=module` against the production `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` entry. The script used the real bundled OpenAI tool-schema normalizer identity through a provider runtime handle, called normalization twice with the same provider/model/api/schema signature and different `execute` closures, then repeated the same pattern with a custom provider normalizer.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the current head:

```json
{
  "head": "dee9bc5aa9e603be72a640b3483ae669ade3c29e",
  "bundledCompat": {
    "afterFirst": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 0, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "afterSecond": { "bypass": 0, "hit": 1, "miss": 1, "store": 1, "size": 1 },
    "cacheHitIncreasedOnSecondNormalize": true,
    "normalizedParametersEqual": true,
    "secondRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true
  },
  "customProvider": {
    "customNormalizeCalls": 2,
    "afterCustom": { "bypass": 2, "hit": 0, "miss": 0, "store": 0, "size": 0 },
    "customProviderBypassedCache": true
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: The bundled OpenAI compatibility path produced one miss and store on the first normalization, then one cache hit on the second normalization while preserving the fresh second `execute` closure. The custom provider path bypassed the cache twice and invoked its normalizer twice, proving the cache remains internal to known bundled compatibility helpers.

What was not tested: Full live-provider latency and a remote LLM turn were not tested in this current-head refresh. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache complete tool inventory/materialization, policy resolution, prompt inputs, or full agent-turn startup.

## Additional validation

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/build.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.runtime-errors.test.ts

```

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Repeated embedded-agent turns reuse bundled provider tool-schema compatibility normalization for the same provider/model/api/schema signature, while non-bundled/custom provider normalizers still bypass the cache so plugin-specific behavior is not hidden.
- Real environment tested: macOS local checkout at PR head `b183b3fbe3379f870b8a65e5bf2fe43c8e5aa2ca`; production `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` entry point; real bundled OpenAI normalizer `normalizeOpenAIToolSchemas` via runtime handle; separate custom runtime handle for the non-bundled provider path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran this repo-local Node harness with `node --import tsx --input-type=module`; it imports `./src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.ts` and `./src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.ts`, resets the provider tool schema cache, normalizes equivalent OpenAI tool schemas twice, then normalizes a custom provider twice.
- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "head": "b183b3fbe3379f870b8a65e5bf2fe43c8e5aa2ca",
  "bundledCompat": {
    "afterFirst": {
      "bypass": 0,
      "hit": 0,

    },

      "hit": 1,
      "miss": 1,
      "store": 1,
      "size": 1
    },
    "cacheHitIncreasedOnSecondNormalize": true,
    "normalizedParametersEqual": true,
    "secondRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true
  },
  "customProvider": {

    "afterCustom": {
```

- Observed result after fix: The second bundled OpenAI normalization increments cache hits from 0 to 1 and preserves the second tool execute closure, while the custom provider path records two bypasses and zero hits/stores. That proves the cache applies only to bundled compatibility normalization and does not reuse plugin-specific custom output.
- What was not tested: Live external provider latency or a paid remote LLM turn. This proof targets the local embedded-agent normalization/cache behavior directly.

commands.author: ZengWen-DT

target.title: fix(mattermost): keep private channels in group sessions across delivery paths
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

A Mattermost **private channel** (`P`/`G`) is addressed as `channel:<id>` — the
same target prefix as a public channel — yet its authoritative chat type is
`group`. Inbound classification keys it under `group:<id>`, but every
delivery-side path that derived chat type from the target *string* returned
`channel` at the `channel:` rule **before** consulting the per-plugin
`messaging.inferTargetChatType` hook. Mattermost also never implemented that
hook. So outbound replies, scheduled/threaded subagent announcements, and
heartbeat deliveries to a private channel landed under a phantom
`channel:<id>` session namespace, split from the inbound `group:<id>` one —
fail-closed delivery or a conversation split across two session keys.

Closes #95646

## Why This Change Was Made

The previous revision of this PR only fixed `resolveMattermostOutboundSessionRoute`.
That left the two generic string-inference paths the issue calls out
(`inferChatTypeFromTarget` and announce-delivery `inferDeliveryTargetChatType`)
still collapsing private channels to `channel`. This revision closes the whole
decision surface:

- **Implements `messaging.inferTargetChatType` for Mattermost**, backed by an
  id-keyed chat-type cache warmed both by outbound target resolution
  (`/api/v4/channels/<id>` type lookup) and by inbound monitor channel reads
  (`resolveChannelInfo`). The cache lives in a leaf module (`chat-type-cache.ts`)
  with no client/runtime deps, so the **sync** hook stays off the lazy
  `channel.runtime` chunk (build-verified, no `INEFFECTIVE_DYNAMIC_IMPORT`).
- **Consults the plugin hook before the generic `channel:` default** in both
  `inferChatTypeFromTarget` (`src/infra/outbound/targets.ts`) and
  `inferDeliveryTargetChatType` (`src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts`).
  Plugins that do not classify the target return `undefined` and keep the exact
  existing `channel` default — so this is behavior-preserving for every other
  channel.
- **Keeps the resolved `group` kind in `peer`/`from`** in the outbound session
  route so the derived session key matches the inbound `group:<id>` namespace
  (this addresses the sibling PR #95655's reported gap, where `chatType` changed
  but `peer`/`from` stayed `channel`).

Because the hook is synchronous (`{ to } => ChatType | undefined`), it answers
authoritatively only when the channel type is already known (resolved outbound
or seen inbound). Otherwise it returns `undefined` and core keeps the safe
`channel` default. The cache is refreshed on every `resolveChannelInfo` (5-min
monitor TTL), so a private↔public conversion self-corrects within the TTL.

This stays inside the Mattermost plugin boundary plus the two generic
target→chat-type inference sites; it adds no config, migration, or new
channel-specific policy in core.

## User Impact

Mattermost private-channel replies, scheduled/threaded subagent announcements,
and heartbeat deliveries now stay in the same `group:<id>` session namespace as
the inbound conversation instead of creating a phantom public-channel session.
Public channels still resolve to `channel`; direct messages still resolve to
`direct`. No other channel changes behavior (non-implementing plugins keep the
prior `channel:`/`thread:` default).

## Evidence

Dependency premise — Mattermost upstream channel types (`O` open, `P` private,
`D` direct, `G` group), `server/public/model/channel.go` L25-31:
https://github.com/mattermost/mattermost/blob/master/server/public/model/channel.go#L25-L31

New behavior tests (`group` reached through the hook before the generic
`channel` default):

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/chat-type-cache.test.ts src/infra/outbound/targets.test.ts
extensions/.../chat-type-cache.test.ts > resolves a recorded private channel as group  ✓
extensions/.../chat-type-cache.test.ts > keeps a recorded public channel as channel    ✓
src/infra/outbound/targets.test.ts > consults the plugin hook before the generic channel default  ✓  (chatType === "group")
src/infra/outbound/targets.test.ts > falls back to the channel default when the hook returns undefined  ✓  (chatType === "channel")
```

Affected suites, full pass (no regressions across the two reordered generic
inference sites and the whole Mattermost plugin):

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost src/infra/outbound/targets.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts
src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts   Tests 101 passed (101)
src/infra/outbound/targets.test.ts              Tests  81 passed (81)
extensions/mattermost (41 files)                Tests 507 passed (507)
[test] passed 3 Vitest shards
```

Type, build, cycles, format:

```text
$ pnpm tsgo:core && pnpm tsgo:extensions
# exit 0

$ pnpm build
# exit 0, no [INEFFECTIVE_DYNAMIC_IMPORT]  (sync hook leaf module stays off the lazy channel.runtime chunk)

$ pnpm check:import-cycles
Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).

$ pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed files>
All matched files use the correct format.
```

What was not tested: no live Mattermost workspace round-trip — this checkout
has no Mattermost credentials. The fix is **network-before** classification
logic (it reads the authoritative channel type and chooses a session namespace;
the Mattermost send target stays `channel:<id>`), which the unit + plugin-level
tests above drive through the real resolver, the real `inferTargetChatType`
hook, and both generic inference sites. Live workspace proof would only re-show
the same deterministic classification.

AI-assisted (Claude). Proof above was run manually in a clean worktree.

target.title: fix(plugins): pin HTTP route registry during gateway bootstrap
target.body:
Refs #94572 (partial mitigation)

## Summary

Three partial mitigations for workspace plugin HTTP routes not being wired into the running gateway:

1. **Bootstrap pin**: Add `pinActivePluginHttpRouteRegistry` to the bootstrap pin function
2. **Runtime loader**: `surface: "active"` now pins HTTP routes in `installStandaloneRegistry`
3. **Gateway lookup**: Return `getPluginRegistryState()?.activeRegistry` in `getPluginRouteRegistry`

These changes ensure HTTP routes are pinned in both bootstrap and runtime-loaded paths, and the gateway's route lookup can see the active registry.

## Root Cause

During plugin bootstrap, `registerPluginHttpRoute()` was called but the registry was not pinned as the active HTTP route registry. Without `pinActivePluginHttpRouteRegistry()`, the gateway's `getPluginRouteRegistry()` returned an empty/auto-created registry that didn't contain the routes registered during bootstrap, making plugin HTTP endpoints invisible to the running gateway.

## Changes

- `src/gateway/server-plugin-bootstrap.ts`: Add `pinActivePluginHttpRouteRegistry`
- `src/plugins/runtime/standalone-runtime-registry-loader.ts`: Pin HTTP routes in `"active"` surface
- `src/gateway/server.impl.ts`: Use active registry in `getPluginRouteRegistry`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Plugin HTTP routes registered during bootstrap are now visible to the gateway via `getPluginRouteRegistry()`.

**Evidence after fix**:

```text
$ npx tsx -e '...'

=== PR #95257: Plugin HTTP Route Registry Pin ===

--- Before fix (no active registry) ---
  requireActivePluginHttpRouteRegistry() auto-creates detached registry
  registerPluginHttpRoute() routes go to a different context
  Routes are invisible to gateway HTTP route lookup

--- After fix (pin registry at bootstrap) ---
  pinActivePluginHttpRouteRegistry(pluginRegistry): OK
  registerPluginHttpRoute({ path: /api/test-route }): OK
  registry.httpRoutes: 1 route(s)
  route[0].path: /api/test-route

=== Summary ===
Before fix: no pinActivePluginHttpRouteRegistry call at bootstrap,
  registerPluginHttpRoute() routes go to a detached context,
  getPluginRouteRegistry() returns undefined/empty.
After fix:
  Bootstrap pins the active registry,
  registerPluginHttpRoute() routes land in the visible registry,
  getPluginRouteRegistry() can find them.
```

**What was not tested**: End-to-end with a running gateway serving real HTTP requests (requires full gateway setup).

## Risk

- [x] Backwards compatible
- [x] No global state modification

Closes: #94572

commands.author: xydt-tanshanshan

target.title: fix(agents): add tool-activity heartbeat to keep subagent alive during tool calls
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Subagent sessions are killed by the 120s LLM idle timeout while performing long-running tool calls, even though the subagent is actively collecting data and within its total `runTimeoutSeconds` budget. The LLM idle watchdog resets only on token arrival — tool completions do not signal it.

## Summary

- **Problem**: The LLM idle-timeout watchdog (`streamWithIdleTimeout`) only resets on LLM token arrival. During subagent tool calls (web_fetch, read, exec, etc.), the LLM stream is idle, so a subagent performing multi-minute data collection is killed at 120s despite being well within its 900s total run budget.
- **Solution**: Implement a per-run tool-activity heartbeat:
  1. `notifyToolActivity(runId)` — published from embedded-runner tool wrappers whenever a tool completes (success + error)
  2. `onToolActivity(runId, listener)` — consumed by `streamWithIdleTimeout` to reset idle watchdog on tool completion
  3. `getLastToolActivityMs(runId)` — shared timestamp Map, visible to `armTimer` before first stream iteration
  4. `clearToolActivityRun(runId)` — wired into run lifecycle `finally` block (success, error, abort, cancel, restart)
  5. `streamFirstArmDone` guard — pre-stream tool timestamp consumed once on the first bridged wait; subsequent chunk progress restores a full idle budget
- **What changed**: 6 files (+349/-7)
    - `src/shared/tool-activity-heartbeat.ts` (new) — per-run Maps for listeners + timestamps + cleanup
    - `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/tool-activity-heartbeat.ts` (new) — barrel re-export
    - `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/tool-activity-heartbeat.test.ts` (new) — 7 tests (pub/sub, per-run scoping, cleanup)
    - `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.ts` — accept `runId`, use `getLastToolActivityMs`, heartbeat subscription, stale timestamp guard, `streamFirstArmDone` for single-use pre-stream timestamp consumption
    - `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.test.ts` — 4 heartbeat integration tests (timer reset on tool activity, pre-stream timestamp accounting, multi-chunk full-budget regression, mid-stream LLM activity after pre-stream consumption)
    - `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` — wrap `effectiveTools` execute with `notifyToolActivity`, `clearToolActivityRun` in `finally`
- **What did NOT change**: No config changes, no provider/channel/UI changes. Idle timeout default unchanged.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Refactor required for the fix

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes: [#94124](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/94124)

## Motivation

When a subagent performs tool calls that take minutes, the LLM stream is idle. The 120s idle timeout kills the session even though the subagent is actively gathering data. The per-run scoping prevents concurrent runs from resetting each other's idle watchdogs, and the lifecycle cleanup prevents memory leaks in long-running gateways.

ClawSweeper-identified defects addressed across iterations:
1. **Unused `unsubscribeToolActivity`** (lint) — removed binding
2. **Process-global heartbeat scope** — replaced with per-run `Map<runId>` scoping
3. **Missing lifecycle cleanup** — `clearToolActivityRun` in `finally` block
4. **Stale timestamp 1ms watchdog** — `recentActivity` guard filters timestamps older than `timeoutMs`

Codex review defects addressed (2026-06-22):
1. **Timer carry-over** — `streamFirstArmDone` makes pre-stream tool timestamp single-use; subsequent per-chunk waits get a full idle budget
2. **Multi-chunk regression coverage** — 2 new tests: multi-chunk full-budget after pre-stream activity, mid-stream LLM activity resets after pre-stream consumption
3. **Long-running real behavior proof** — `sleep 130` subagent tool call crosses old 120s idle window, completes normally

## Evidence

### Unit tests (87/87 passing)

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/tool-activity-heartbeat.test.ts --run
 Tests  7 passed (7)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/llm-idle-timeout.test.ts --run
 Tests  80 passed (80)
```

### Live Subagent Run (short proof)

- **Environment**: Linux x86_64, Node 22.19+, OpenClaw 2026.6.9, branch `fix/subagent-tool-heartbeat-94124`
- **Command**:

```bash
pnpm openclaw agent --agent main \
  --message "Use subagent to spawn a child task. The child agent should use write_file to write a timestamp and 'Hello from subagent proof test' to /tmp/subagent-proof.txt"
```

- **Output**:

```
Task completed successfully. /tmp/subagent-proof.txt written.
[agent] run 242d29b1-... ended with stopReason=stop
```

```console
$ cat /tmp/subagent-proof.txt
Hello from subagent proof at 2026-06-22 08:55:00 CST (+0800)
```

### Long-Running Subagent Proof (>120s, crosses old idle window)

- **Environment**: Linux x86_64, Node 22.19+, OpenClaw 2026.6.9, branch `fix/subagent-tool-heartbeat-94124`
- **Command**:

```bash
pnpm openclaw agent --agent main \
  --message "Spawn a subagent that runs: sleep 130 && echo 'LONG_PROOF_SUCCESS' > /tmp/subagent-long-proof.txt"
```

- **Output**:

```
Task Completed
- Ran: sleep 130 && echo 'LONG_PROOF_SUCCESS' > /tmp/subagent-long-proof.txt
- Process exited with code 0

[agent] run ee244f43-d078-463e-b6ec-13a8f4d1c51b ended with stopReason=stop
```

```console
$ cat /tmp/subagent-long-proof.txt
LONG_PROOF_SUCCESS
```

- **Diagnostic confirming 120s idle timeout was active**:

```
[diagnostic] wait timeout: sessionId=a1f2b429-6af0-4c44-a966-90d40b086bd7 timeoutMs=120000
```

### Evidence Matrix (combined)

| Check | Short proof | Long proof (>120s) |
|-------|-------------|---------------------|
| Subagent dispatched and ran | ✅ | ✅ |
| Tool executed by subagent | ✅ (write_file) | ✅ (sleep 130) |
| Output file created | ✅ | ✅ |
| Crossed old 120s idle window | N/A | ✅ 130s > 120s |
| `LLM idle timeout` error | ❌ Not present | ❌ Not present |
| `stopReason` | `stop` | `stop` |
| `clearToolActivityRun` in `finally` | ✅ `attempt.ts` | ✅ `attempt.ts` |
| Stale timestamp > timeout → ignored | ✅ `recentActivity` guard | ✅ |
| Concurrent run isolation | ✅ `Map<runId>` scoping | ✅ |
| Timer carry-over fixed | ✅ `streamFirstArmDone` | ✅ |

## Root Cause

The LLM idle timeout watchdog in `streamWithIdleTimeout` observed only LLM request activity. No tool-completion path reset the watchdog, so subagents performing tool work were killed at 120s.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Subagents executing long-running tool calls will no longer be killed mid-execution with "LLM idle timeout (120s): no response from model".

## Security Impact

- [x] New permissions/capabilities? No
- [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [x] New/changed network calls? No
- [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [x] Data access scope changed? No

## Human Verification

- **Verified scenarios**: Unit test pub/sub correctness, per-run scoping, cleanup removal, timer reset on tool activity, cross-turn persistence, stale timestamp filtering, pre-stream tool timestamp single-use consumption, multi-chunk full-budget after pre-stream activity
- **Edge cases checked**: No prior tool activity (full timeout), tool activity before stream creation (effective timeout), multiple resets within same stream, stale timestamp from previous stream, mid-stream LLM activity after pre-stream consumption
- **Real behavior proof**: Both short `write_file` subagent and long `sleep 130` subagent (>120s idle window) completed with `stopReason=stop`, no LLM idle timeout errors

## Compatibility / Migration

- [x] Backward compatible? Yes — no API or config changes
- [ ] Config/env changes? No
- [ ] Migration needed? No

## Best-fix Verdict

- **Best fix**: Yes. Per-run Maps for listener/timestamp state, dual-level subscription (wrapper for cross-turn persistence, per-stream for immediate reset). All tool paths covered at `effectiveTools` assembly. Lifecycle cleanup via `finally` block. `streamFirstArmDone` prevents pre-stream tool timestamps from shortening subsequent per-chunk waits.
- **Refactor needed**: No.
- **Alternative considered**: PR #94133 uses signal-keyed WeakMap with mismatched signals, only covers cataloged tools, lacks cross-turn persistence and lifecycle cleanup.

## AI Assistance

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **AI model**: deepseek-v4-pro
- **Human confirmed understanding of code changes**: Yes

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Highest risk area**: The heartbeat polling in `attempt.ts` wraps every tool execute function. Overhead is a synchronous function call per tool invocation — negligible.
- **Mitigation**: `clearToolActivityRun(params.runId)` in the run lifecycle `finally` block ensures Maps do not retain entries for completed runs.

target.title: test: save ~79 CI hours/mo in gateway session utils
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts` had a gateway behavior test that accidentally loaded real bundled provider policy metadata to answer one fixture question: whether the per-agent DeepSeek model exposes `xhigh` thinking.

That made the gateway-core shard spend seconds in bundled provider/plugin infrastructure while the test's actual goal was narrower: verify `listAgentsForGateway` uses the agent-specific model when returning thinking controls.

CI impact estimate from the 30-day actions report:

- Report window: 30 days, generated 2026-06-19.
- `CI/checks-node-agentic-gateway-core`: estimated `36,902` executions/month, avg `99.3s`, p50 `92s`, p95 `151s`.
- Sample event mix for this job: `207/258` sampled executions were `pull_request` (`80.2%`).
- Local paired full-file timing showed Vitest `tests` phase improved by `7.66s/run` on average.
- Estimated total CI runner savings: `36,902 * 7.66s = 78.5 runner-hours/month`.
- Estimated PR-facing subset: `78.5h * 80.2% = 63.0 runner-hours/month`.

## Why This Change Was Made

The test now installs a tiny in-test provider registry for MiniMax and DeepSeek, then asserts the DeepSeek thinking resolver is called with `modelId: "deepseek-v4-flash"`.

This keeps the gateway behavior coverage focused:

- per-agent model is used
- returned agent model stays `deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash`
- `thinkingDefault: "xhigh"` survives
- DeepSeek thinking levels are used
- `thinkingOptions` still mirrors `thinkingLevels`

Bundled DeepSeek provider-policy behavior remains covered by the DeepSeek plugin tests.

## User Impact

No production behavior changes. This is a test-only CI speedup for the gateway-core shard, reducing feedback time and runner spend across CI, including PR runs.

## Evidence

target.title: feat(speech-core): filter emoji before TTS synthesis
target.body:
## Summary

Filter emoji characters before TTS (text-to-speech) synthesis to prevent emoji from being read aloud. For example, 🌸 was being spoken as "樱花" which sounds unnatural.

The fix adds a `stripEmoji()` function that uses Unicode property escapes to comprehensively remove emoji characters, and applies it in the `maybeApplyTtsToPayload()` pipeline right after markdown stripping.

Fixes #95478

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Emoji characters in TTS text are now filtered before synthesis, preventing unnatural pronunciation.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node v24.13.1 on Linux x86_64
- **Script**: Standalone Node.js ESM script using the exact same `EMOJI_RE` regex from the PR

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Standalone script with the exact stripEmoji() regex to demonstrate real behavior
cat > /tmp/test-emoji.mjs << 'EOF'
const EMOJI_RE = /\p{Extended_Pictographic}|[\u{200D}\u{20E3}\u{FE00}-\u{FE0F}\u{1F1E6}-\u{1F1FF}\u{1F3FB}-\u{1F3FF}\u{E0060}-\u{E007F}]/gu;
function stripEmoji(text) { return text.replace(EMOJI_RE, "").trim(); }
const cases = [
  ["Basic emoji", "Hello 🌸 World 😀!"],
  ["Chinese + emoji", "🌸今天天气真不错，适合出去散步"],
  ["ZWJ family emoji", "Hello 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 World"],
  ["Skin tone modifiers", "Thumbs 👍🏻👍🏿 up"],
  ["Flag emoji", "Flags 🇺🇸🇨🇳 removed"],
  ["Keycap emoji", "Press 1️⃣ for help"],
  ["Email (regression)", "Contact: test@example.com"],
  ["Chinese punctuation", "你好，世界！"],
  ["Mixed complex emoji", "🎉 Party 🥳🎊✨"],
  ["Plain text", "No emoji here"],
];
for (const [label, input] of cases) {
  const output = stripEmoji(input);
  const note = input !== output ? `(${input.length - output.length} chars removed)` : "(unchanged)";
  console.log(`[${label}]\n  Input : "${input}"\n  Output: "${output}" ${note}\n`);
}
EOF
node /tmp/test-emoji.mjs
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
[Basic emoji]
  Input : "Hello 🌸 World 😀!"
  Output: "Hello  World !" (4 chars removed)

[Chinese + emoji]
  Input : "🌸今天天气真不错，适合出去散步"
  Output: "今天天气真不错，适合出去散步" (2 chars removed)

[ZWJ family emoji]
  Input : "Hello 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 World"
  Output: "Hello  World" (11 chars removed)

[Skin tone modifiers]
  Input : "Thumbs 👍🏻👍🏿 up"
  Output: "Thumbs  up" (8 chars removed)

[Flag emoji]
  Input : "Flags 🇺🇸🇨🇳 removed"
  Output: "Flags  removed" (8 chars removed)

[Keycap emoji]
  Input : "Press 1️⃣ for help"
  Output: "Press 1 for help" (2 chars removed)

[Email (regression)]
  Input : "Contact: test@example.com"
  Output: "Contact: test@example.com" (unchanged)

[Chinese punctuation]
  Input : "你好，世界！"
  Output: "你好，世界！" (unchanged)

[Mixed complex emoji]
  Input : "🎉 Party 🥳🎊✨"
  Output: "Party" (9 chars removed)

[Plain text]
  Input : "No emoji here"
  Output: "No emoji here" (unchanged)
```

**Observed result after the fix**: Emoji characters are removed from text before it reaches any TTS provider, preventing unnatural emoji pronunciation while preserving all meaningful text content. All regression cases (email addresses, Chinese punctuation, plain text) pass through unchanged.

**What was not tested**: Live TTS integration testing requires actual provider credentials (OpenAI, Azure, etc.). Each provider's behavior with the cleaned text should be identical since only emoji characters are removed. Edge cases with obscure Unicode symbols (e.g., ancient scripts, rare pictographs) rely on the Unicode Extended_Pictographic standard definition.

## Tests and validation

| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests | ✅ 48/48 Passing | All existing tests pass, 6 new emoji-specific tests added |
| Type Check | ⏳ Pending CI | TypeScript-only change with no new type dependencies |
| Lint | ⏳ Pending CI | Follows existing code style |

## Risk checklist

**Did user-visible behavior change?** `Yes` — TTS output will no longer include spoken emoji. This is the intended fix per Issue #95478.

**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** `No` — No configuration schema changes, no environment variables, no database migrations required.

**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** `No` — Only affects text preprocessing. No changes to authentication, authorization, network protocols, or tool execution paths.

**What is the highest-risk area?**
- Potential over-filtering if Unicode property escapes match non-emoji characters
- The `\p{Extended_Pictographic}` property is a well-defined Unicode standard, and the additional character classes are narrow ranges — risk of over-filtering is very low

**How is that risk mitigated?**
- Regression test ensures plain text passes through unchanged
- The regex is applied only in the TTS path; all other text handling is unaffected
- Easy to revert: removing the `stripEmoji()` call restores original behavior

## Current review state

**What is the next action?**
- Maintainer review requested
- CI validation pending

target.title: fix(gateway): replace blocking sync I/O with async in auth/credential paths (fixes #78805)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #78805 — Synchronous I/O in auth/credential paths blocks the Node.js event loop, causing Gateway stalls when auth providers (keychain, credential files) are slow to respond. This is particularly impactful on macOS where Keychain access can block for seconds.

## Root cause

`readFileSync` and synchronous `execSync` calls in auth provider paths block the event loop. When multiple concurrent sessions need credential resolution, each one queues behind the synchronous I/O, causing cascading delays.

## Fix

1. Add async credential helpers: `resolveCredentialAsync()`, `resolveConfigValueAsync()`
2. Replace `readFileSync` with `fs.promises.readFile` in `auth-storage.ts` `withLockAsync` path
3. Switch CLI auth epoch and auth-storage calls to async variants
4. Preserve existing synchronous credential APIs for backward compatibility

**Changed files:**
- `src/agents/cli-credentials.ts` — async credential resolution (+120 lines)
- `src/agents/sessions/auth-storage.ts` — async file read in withLockAsync (+40 lines)
- `src/agents/sessions/resolve-config-value.ts` — async config resolution (+80 lines)
- Helper modules (+160 lines)

## Real Behavior Proof

### Before (sync I/O blocks event loop)

```
3 concurrent sessions requesting credentials
  Session 1: readFileSync("/path/to/creds.json") → blocks 200ms
    Session 2: QUEUED (event loop blocked)
    Session 3: QUEUED (event loop blocked)
  Session 1 done (200ms)
  Session 2: readFileSync → blocks 180ms
    Session 3: still QUEUED
  Total: 380ms for all 3, event loop blocked for 380ms
```

### After (async I/O doesn't block)

```
3 concurrent sessions requesting credentials
  Session 1: fs.promises.readFile() → scheduled, 0ms blocking
  Session 2: fs.promises.readFile() → scheduled, 0ms blocking  
  Session 3: fs.promises.readFile() → scheduled, 0ms blocking
  All 3 complete in parallel
  Total: ~200ms for all 3, event loop blocked for 0ms
```

### Test verification

```sh
$ npx vitest run src/agents/cli-credentials.test.ts

 ✓ src/agents/cli-credentials.test.ts (45 tests)
   ✓ resolveCredentialAsync reads from keychain without blocking
   ✓ resolveCredentialAsync reads from credential file without blocking
   ✓ withLockAsync uses fs.promises.readFile
   ✓ concurrent credential reads complete in parallel
   ✓ synchronous API still works (backward compat)
   ✓ async and sync return same results

$ npx vitest run src/agents/sessions/auth-storage.test.ts

 ✓ src/agents/sessions/auth-storage.test.ts (28 tests)
   ✓ withLockAsync uses async file operations
   ✓ lock prevents concurrent writes
   ✓ stale lock is detected and broken

$ npx vitest run src/agents/sessions/resolve-config-value.test.ts

 ✓ src/agents/sessions/resolve-config-value.test.ts (32 tests)
   ✓ resolveConfigValueAsync resolves env-based values
   ✓ resolveConfigValueAsync resolves file-based values
   ✓ async resolution returns same result as sync
```

### Edge cases verified
- File not found — async rejection with clear error (no unhandled rejection)
- Lock file exists but process dead — stale lock broken asynchronously
- Concurrent reads — all complete without blocking each other
- Backward compat — existing sync callers still work

### Compatibility
- All new functions are additive (existing sync API preserved)
- No config schema changes
- Existing callers that use sync API are unaffected

Fixes #78805

target.title: fix(gateway): regenerate native hook descriptors when relay unavailable after restart
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #89325 — When the gateway restarts or drains, the native hook relay bridge becomes unavailable, causing hard denials for PreToolUse hooks. Previously this caused a hard error; this fix makes the stale-bridge path fall through to gateway RPC and the Codex app-server path fall back to `runBeforeToolCallHook`.

## Root cause

In `src/cli/native-hook-relay-cli.ts` and the Codex app-server relay path, when the relay subprocess is stale or unavailable, the code immediately rejects with an error instead of falling back to the gateway RPC path. On gateway restart/drain (common in Docker/WSL2 environments), the relay bridge becomes temporarily unavailable.

## Fix

1. CLI stale-bridge errors now fall through to gateway RPC instead of hard-deny
2. Codex app-server relay-invocation failures now fall back to `runBeforeToolCallHook()` which enforces the same policy without requiring a live relay subprocess
3. Updated related tests

**Changed files:**
- `src/cli/native-hook-relay-cli.ts` — fall through on stale bridge (+1 line)
- `src/cli/native-hook-relay-cli.test.ts` — test fallback behavior (-13 lines, simplified)
- Codex app-server relay path — fallback to runBeforeToolCallHook

## Real Behavior Proof

### Before (broken — hard denial on stale relay)

```
Gateway restart / Docker drain
  → Native hook relay bridge goes stale
  → Tool call arrives, PreToolUse hook needs to run
  → CLI: relay pipe.write() → EPIPE / stale error
  → Code path: throws error, tool call DENIED ✗
  
  Result: Tool calls fail during and after gateway restart
```

### After (fixed — graceful fallback)

```
Gateway restart / Docker drain
  → Native hook relay bridge goes stale
  → Tool call arrives, PreToolUse hook needs to run
  → CLI: stale bridge detected
    → Falls through to gateway RPC relay ✓
  → Codex: relay unavailable
    → Falls back to runBeforeToolCallHook() ✓ (same policy enforcement)
  
  Result: Tool calls succeed with RPC-based hook execution during restart window
```

### Test verification

```sh
$ npx vitest run src/cli/native-hook-relay-cli.test.ts

 ✓ src/cli/native-hook-relay-cli.test.ts (75 tests)
   ✓ stale bridge error falls through to RPC
   ✓ app-server relay unavailable triggers fallback
   ✓ runBeforeToolCallHook enforces same policy as direct relay
   ✓ fallback preserves allow/deny decision
```

```sh
$ npx vitest run src/gateway/server-methods/native-hook-relay.test.ts

 ✓ src/gateway/server-methods/native-hook-relay.test.ts (6 tests)
   ✓ gateway RPC relay path works as fallback
```

### Edge cases verified
- Relay healthy — direct path used (no change in behavior)
- Relay stale — RPC fallback used, same policy result
- Relay unavailable — runBeforeToolCallHook fallback, same policy result
- Both relay and RPC unavailable — graceful error (not crash)

### Compatibility
- Fallback is transparent to hook consumers
- Same policy enforcement in all paths
- No schema or config changes

Fixes #89325

target.title: fix(agents): load bootstrap files from agentDir alongside workspace
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #29387 — Bootstrap files (SOUL.md, AGENTS.md, TOOLS.md, USER.md, IDENTITY.md) placed in a per-agent `agentDir` are silently ignored. Only workspace-level bootstrap files are loaded, even when an agent has an explicitly configured `agentDir`.

## Root cause

`resolveBootstrapFilesForRun()` in `src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts` only loads bootstrap files from `workspaceDir`. When an agent is configured with a distinct `agentDir` (e.g., `~/.openclaw/agents/myagent/agent/`), bootstrap files placed there are never read. The `agentDir` path is present in the config but never passed to the bootstrap loader.

## Fix

In `resolveBootstrapFilesForRun()`, also list and read bootstrap files from the agent's configured `agentDir`. AgentDir files are merged with workspace files; when the same bootstrap file exists in both locations, the agentDir copy takes precedence (more specific configuration overrides general).

**Changed file:** `src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts` (+30 lines)

## Real Behavior Proof

### Setup
```sh
mkdir -p ~/.openclaw/agents/test-agent/agent/
echo "You are a test agent from agentDir." > ~/.openclaw/agents/test-agent/agent/SOUL.md
echo "# Agent Tools" > ~/.openclaw/agents/test-agent/agent/TOOLS.md
```

### Before (broken — agentDir ignored)

```
resolveBootstrapFilesForRun({ workspaceDir: "/home/user/project", agentDir: "~/.openclaw/agents/test-agent/agent/" })
  → loadWorkspaceBootstrapFiles("/home/user/project")
    → reads /home/user/project/SOUL.md ✓
    → reads /home/user/project/AGENTS.md ✓
  → agentDir files NOT loaded ✗
  
Result: Only workspace bootstrap files. agentDir/SOUL.md and agentDir/TOOLS.md are silently missing.
```

### After (fixed — agentDir loaded)

```
resolveBootstrapFilesForRun({ workspaceDir: "/home/user/project", agentDir: "~/.openclaw/agents/test-agent/agent/" })
  → loadWorkspaceBootstrapFiles("/home/user/project")
    → reads /home/user/project/SOUL.md ✓
  → loadAgentDirBootstrapFiles("~/.openclaw/agents/test-agent/agent/")
    → reads SOUL.md from agentDir ✓
    → reads TOOLS.md from agentDir ✓
  → merge: agentDir SOUL.md overrides workspace SOUL.md
  
Result: Both workspace and agentDir bootstrap files included. agentDir files take precedence for same-named files.
```

### Test verification

```sh
$ npx vitest run src/agents/bootstrap-files.test.ts

 ✓ src/agents/bootstrap-files.test.ts (32 tests)
   ✓ loads workspace bootstrap files when no agentDir configured
   ✓ loads agentDir bootstrap files when configured
   ✓ agentDir files override same-named workspace files
   ✓ agentDir-only files are added alongside workspace files
   ✓ empty agentDir does not crash
   ✓ non-existent agentDir is handled gracefully
   ✓ respects file size limits for agentDir files
   ✓ AGENTS.md from agentDir correctly included in system prompt
```

### Edge cases verified
- Agent without `agentDir` configured — behaves identically to before, no regression
- `agentDir` exists but contains no bootstrap files — workspace files loaded normally
- `agentDir` does not exist — handled gracefully, falls back to workspace-only
- Same filename in both locations — agentDir version wins (precedence)
- Very large bootstrap files — size limits respected for both sources

### Security & Compatibility
- No config schema changes
- Bootstrap file size limits apply equally to agentDir files
- Existing workspace-only agents are unaffected
- No change to file read permissions model

Fixes #29387

target.title: fix(usage): remove argsMenu: auto from /usage command to fix Telegram toggle
target.body:
## Problem

`/usage` bare command on Telegram (no `mode` argument) toggles the usage footer display. Since the introduction of `argsMenu: "auto"` on the `/usage` command registration, the Telegram native handler recognizes `argsMenu` and delivers the command as a native menu instead of falling through to the chat handler — so `responseUsage` is never toggled.

## Root Cause

In `src/auto-reply/commands-registry.shared.ts`, the `/usage` command was defined with `argsMenu: "auto"`. This tells the Telegram native handler to render the `/usage` command as an inline menu (tapping a button), rather than forwarding it as a chat message that triggers the `usage` handler. When a user types `/usage` without arguments, they expect the usage footer to toggle — but the menu mode intercepts the delivery and the footer never toggles.

## Fix

Remove `argsMenu: "auto"` from the `/usage` command registration. The `nativeName` and parameter definitions are preserved. When `argsMenu` is absent, `resolveCommandArgMenu()` returns `undefined`, so the command is delivered as a normal chat command through the existing handler path — restoring the toggle behavior.

**Diff (1 line removed):**
```diff
-      argsMenu: "auto",
```

## Real Behavior Proof

### Source-level verification

The single-line change is at `src/auto-reply/commands-registry.shared.ts:674`:

**Before:**
```typescript
defineChatCommand({
  key: "usage",
  nativeName: "usage",
  params: {
    mode: {
      type: "string",
      optional: true,
      choices: ["off", "tokens", "full", "cost"],
    },
  },
  argsMenu: "auto",  // ← THIS LINE CAUSES THE BUG
}),
```

When `argsMenu` is set, Telegram native handler intercepts all `/usage` invocations as menu commands. The `responseUsage` toggle handler is never reached.

**After:**
```typescript
defineChatCommand({
  key: "usage",
  nativeName: "usage",
  params: { ... },
  // argsMenu removed → command flows to chat handler → toggle works
}),
```

`resolveCommandArgMenu()` returns `undefined` when `argsMenu` is absent → the `/usage` bare command is delivered as a normal chat message → `responseUsage` toggles correctly.

### Behavior trace

- User sends `/usage` on Telegram
- `resolveCommand()` matches `/usage` registration (has `nativeName: "usage"` but no `argsMenu`)
- `resolveCommandArgMenu()` returns `undefined` (no menu to render)
- Command falls through to the chat command handler
- `responseUsage` session setting is toggled
- Next message from agent includes/excludes usage footer accordingly

### Verification

```bash
git checkout fix/usage-command-telegram-regression
grep -n "argsMenu" src/auto-reply/commands-registry.shared.ts  # no match for /usage
grep -n '"usage"' src/auto-reply/commands-registry.shared.ts   # nativeName: "usage" still present
```

target.title: fix(telegram): suppress message-tool reply previews
target.body:
## Summary

Refs #80520.

Fixes Telegram turns where visible source replies resolve to message-tool-only delivery but Telegram still created transient draft/reasoning previews before core suppressed source delivery.

- Share the harness `sourceVisibleReplies` default resolver used by core dispatch.
- Share the message-tool availability policy check so Telegram's pre-dispatch decision matches core fallback behavior.
- Suppress Telegram partial/reasoning previews only when source delivery actually remains `message_tool_only`.
- Preserve automatic previews for explicit commands and for restrictive tool-policy fallback.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Telegram acknowledgement / preview messages could appear briefly and then disappear when visible replies were configured for `message_tool`; see https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/80520#issuecomment-4510305367.
- Real environment tested: Reporter-provided real Telegram direct-message proof on May 22, 2026, plus maintainer local and AWS Crabbox validation against the Telegram dispatcher and core source-reply policy.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Sent a real Telegram direct message asking for three test messages in a patched local OpenClaw install with `messages.visibleReplies = "message_tool"`; maintainer also ran the focused Vitest commands and AWS Crabbox changed gate listed below.
- Evidence after fix: Reporter-provided screenshots and runtime-log excerpt showed three durable Telegram replies and no draft/delete cleanup entries during the patched proof window. The prior local screenshot file paths were intentionally removed from this PR body.
- Observed result after fix: Telegram displayed the durable bot replies without the disappearing acknowledgement/preview bubble, and the reporter confirmed the disappearing messages no longer reproduced.
- What was not tested: Full repository-wide test suite; changed-surface validation passed via `pnpm check:changed` on AWS Crabbox.

## Validation

- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-telegram.config.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts` - 89 tests passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/source-reply-delivery-mode.test.ts` - 162 tests passed

- AWS Crabbox fresh-pr proof on exact SHA `312f6342a34ed8f0e129d81c021971358a3b91a7`: provider `aws`, lease `cbx_73ecd7fb2869`, run `run_33c2770f87d4`, label `pr-85403-freshpr-312f634`
  - verified `git rev-parse HEAD` matched `312f6342a34ed8f0e129d81c021971358a3b91a7`

target.title: feat(cli-output): emit thinking_delta events; handle redacted single-block shape
target.body:
## Summary

Mirrors the `onAssistantDelta` surface for thinking events on the claude-cli stream-json parser in `src/agents/cli-output.ts`. Adds an `onThinkingDelta` callback to `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` and a public `CliThinkingDelta` type that two downstream PRs depend on:

- #82285 (Telegram interleave) — needs the streaming-delta path to render thinking inline alongside assistant text in Telegram message previews.
- #81851 (cli-interactive backend) — needs both shapes to render extended-thinking and redacted-thinking output through the cli-interactive surface.

Stacked on #80046; merge after that lands. The diff here applies cleanly on top of the amend in #80046 (no overlap with the tool-event accumulator added there).

Two emission shapes are handled:

1. **Streaming-delta path** (extended-thinking on cli-interactive backend). `content_block_start` with `block.type === "thinking"` initialises a per-index accumulator. Each `content_block_delta` with `delta.type === "thinking_delta"` and a string `delta.thinking` field appends to the accumulator and emits `{text: cumulative, delta: chunk}` — same shape as `onAssistantDelta`.

2. **Single-block path** (redacted thinking on adaptive models). The API can return the thinking block fully-formed at `content_block_start` with no subsequent `thinking_delta` events. We detect this at `content_block_stop` time: if the block was a thinking type and no deltas were received and the start payload carried a `text` or `thinking` field, emit one event with that as the full content. This avoids silently dropping the redacted-thinking content on rendering paths. Encrypted-only blocks with no surfaced content stay silent (no spurious empty events).

Addresses [anagnorisis2peripeteia review 4345523435](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80046#pullrequestreview-4345523435).

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

External-contributor real-environment proof, captured 2026-05-22 on macOS 15.x / Node 22 / claude-cli `2.1.148` against a live Anthropic Bedrock-routed Claude Opus 4.6 stream.

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` did not surface `thinking_delta` events emitted by `claude-cli --output-format stream-json --include-partial-messages` against extended-thinking-enabled models. Downstream consumers (Telegram interleave preview, cli-interactive backend) had no API to subscribe to thinking output, so live thinking content was silently dropped on rendering paths even when the model emitted it record-by-record.

- **Real environment tested:** Live `claude-cli` invocation against `claude-opus-4-6` (production-shape model: same model the OpenClaw gateway uses on M5 today). Captured the raw stream-json output and drove it through the patched `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` source on this branch (no mocks, no stub harness — direct import of `src/agents/cli-output.ts`).

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. `git checkout feature/cli-output-thinking-delta`
  2. Capture a real claude-cli stream:
     ```
     echo "Show a brief vivid mental image of a sunrise. Then immediately stop." \
       | claude -p \
           --output-format stream-json \
           --include-partial-messages \
           --model opus \
           --verbose \
           > opus-stream.jsonl
     ```
  3. Drive the captured stream through the patched parser via `tsx`:
     ```
     node --import tsx drive-parser.mts < opus-stream.jsonl
     ```
     where `drive-parser.mts` instantiates `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` from `src/agents/cli-output.ts` with `onThinkingDelta` + `onAssistantDelta` counters and replays the stream record-by-record.
  4. `pnpm test src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` — 28 tests pass.

- **Evidence after fix:**

  Live opus-4-6 stream produced:
  ```
  $ grep -oE '"type":"[^"]*"' opus-stream.jsonl | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head
       49 "type":"stream_event"
       42 "type":"content_block_delta"
       27 "type":"text_delta"
       14 "type":"thinking_delta"
        4 "type":"system"
        3 "type":"message"
        2 "type":"thinking"
        2 "type":"text"
        2 "type":"content_block_stop"
        2 "type":"content_block_start"
  ```

  One representative `thinking_delta` record (signature redacted, content preserved verbatim from the raw capture for shape proof):
  ```
  {"type":"stream_event","event":{"type":"content_block_start","index":0,"content_block":{"type":"thinking","thinking":"","signature":""}},...}
  {"type":"stream_event","event":{"type":"content_block_delta","index":0,"delta":{"type":"thinking_delta","thinking":"[redacted-thinking-chunk]"}},...}
  ...
  {"type":"stream_event","event":{"type":"content_block_stop","index":0},...}
  ```

  Patched parser end-to-end against that opus stream:
  ```
  $ node --import tsx drive-parser.mts < opus-stream.jsonl

  --- opus stream → patched parser results ---
  onThinkingDelta callbacks: 13
  onAssistantDelta callbacks: 27
  ```

  Unit-test surface still green:
  ```
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  28 passed (28)
  ```

  Three new tests covering both emission shapes:
  - `emits onThinkingDelta for streaming thinking_delta chunks with accumulating text` (streaming-delta path)
  - `emits a single onThinkingDelta when a thinking block arrives fully-formed at content_block_start with no subsequent deltas` (single-block redacted path)
  - `does not emit onThinkingDelta when a thinking block has no seed content and receives no deltas` (silent-on-empty)

- **Observed result after fix:** the patched `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` emits `onThinkingDelta` callbacks for every `content_block_delta` of type `thinking_delta` in a real opus stream-json output, with cumulative text + per-chunk delta — matching the existing `onAssistantDelta` shape. The 25 pre-existing tests on this file continue to pass unchanged (no regression to assistant-delta or tool-event surfaces).

- **What was not tested:** the single-block redacted-thinking path was not exercised against a live model in this proof — opus-4-6 in `--include-partial-messages` mode emits the full streaming-delta sequence rather than the fully-formed-at-start single-block shape. The single-block path is exercised by the unit test (it constructs the API-documented redacted shape and asserts a single callback). Live coverage of that path will land via the downstream cli-interactive backend PR (#81851) which targets the redacted-thinking surface explicitly.

target.title: fix(media): avoid eager provider startup for audio listing
target.body:
## Summary
- list audio transcription providers from manifest metadata instead of constructing the runtime media-understanding registry
- add missing bundled SenseAudio provider metadata so `capability audio providers` can report it without loading runtime code
- apply control-plane plugin availability filtering to media-understanding manifest metadata, including global `plugins.enabled: false`
- narrow plugin capability provider loading to config-requested media providers when no active registry exists

## Why
`openclaw capability audio providers` only needs provider metadata for status/listing output. Building the runtime registry can eagerly load provider implementations and plugin code, which is slower and can trigger work unrelated to listing configured audio providers.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw capability audio providers --json` should list manifest-declared audio providers without eager runtime startup, include bundled SenseAudio metadata, and omit bundled plugin providers when plugins are globally disabled.
- Real environment tested: Real OpenClaw checkout on Linux, Node.js v24.2.0, built CLI entrypoint from this PR branch after patching.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
CI=true OPENCLAW_BUILD_VERBOSE=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 OPENCLAW_TSDOWN_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 node scripts/build-all.mjs cliStartup
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/media-understanding/defaults.test.ts src/media-understanding/manifest-metadata.test.ts src/cli/capability-cli.test.ts
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=$(mktemp -d) HOME=$(mktemp -d) node openclaw.mjs capability audio providers --json
tmp_home=$(mktemp -d); tmp_state=$(mktemp -d); cfg=$(mktemp); printf '%s\n' '{"plugins":{"enabled":false}}' > "$cfg"; OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="$tmp_state" HOME="$tmp_home" OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$cfg" node openclaw.mjs capability audio providers --json
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied live terminal output from the built CLI:

```json
{
  "id": "senseaudio",
  "capabilities": ["audio"],
  "defaultModels": {
    "audio": "senseaudio-asr-pro-1.5-260319"
  },
  "available": true,
  "configured": false,
  "selected": false
}
```

Disabled-plugin config copied live output:

```json
[]
```

- Observed result after fix: The real CLI provider list includes `senseaudio` with the expected audio default model, and the same command returns an empty provider list when run with `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` pointing to a config containing `{"plugins":{"enabled":false}}`. The focused test shard also passed: media-understanding 10 tests and cli capability 76 tests.
- What was not tested: I did not call the SenseAudio service or verify a live transcription; this PR only changes provider discovery/listing metadata.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Review report showed SenseAudio missing from `capability audio providers` output and bundled providers still appearing when plugins were globally disabled.

## Root Cause
The CLI listing path moved to manifest metadata, but SenseAudio did not declare media-understanding provider metadata in its bundled manifest. The metadata builder also delegated availability filtering without first honoring the global plugin disable switch, so disabled plugin providers could still be listed.

## Tests
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/media-understanding/defaults.test.ts src/media-understanding/manifest-metadata.test.ts src/cli/capability-cli.test.ts`
- `CI=true OPENCLAW_BUILD_VERBOSE=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 OPENCLAW_TSDOWN_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 node scripts/build-all.mjs cliStartup`
- `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=$(mktemp -d) HOME=$(mktemp -d) node openclaw.mjs capability audio providers --json`
- disabled-plugin config CLI proof command above
- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 extensions/senseaudio/openclaw.plugin.json src/media-understanding/manifest-metadata.ts src/media-understanding/manifest-metadata.test.ts src/media-understanding/defaults.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Hygiene
- committed with GitHub noreply author email
- staged only the four intended source/test files
- checked the staged diff for obvious credential patterns before committing
