target.title: fix(doctor): honor per-agent bootstrap profile in size check
target.body:
> AI-assisted: drafted with Claude Code. Bug discovery, fix, regression test, and behavior-proof reproducer all verified by the author on Windows 11 + Node 24.

## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw doctor` reports bootstrap-file-size warnings against the
  global `agents.defaults.bootstrap*MaxChars` budget even when the active
  default agent has a per-agent override under `agents.list[].bootstrap*MaxChars`,
  so the percentages, near-limit flags, and remediation tips are wrong for
  any setup that uses the per-agent profile feature added in #69966.
- Solution: thread the resolved `defaultAgentId` into the three call sites in
  `noteBootstrapFileSize` so the per-agent override is honored consistently
  with the rest of the runtime (`prepareCliRunContext`, `runEmbeddedAttempt`,
  `compactEmbeddedPiSessionDirectOnce`, and `/context` reporting), and update
  the remediation tip text to point at both the per-agent and defaults knobs.
- What changed: `src/commands/doctor-bootstrap-size.ts` resolves
  `defaultAgentId` once and passes it to `resolveBootstrapMaxChars`,
  `resolveBootstrapTotalMaxChars`, and `resolveBootstrapContextForRun`; the
  two truncation tips now mention `agents.list[].bootstrap*MaxChars` first
  with `agents.defaults.*` as fallback. `src/commands/doctor-bootstrap-size.test.ts`
  gains a regression test that asserts the agent id is threaded through, and
  the existing tip-text assertion is updated.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no behavior change for setups that
  rely solely on `agents.defaults.*` (the per-agent resolver falls back to
  defaults). No changes to budget math, warning thresholds, doctor command
  wiring, or other call sites (those were already correct after #69966).
  Doctor still inspects only the default agent's workspace; covering
  non-default agents is out of scope for this fix.

## Motivation

`feat(agents): support per-agent bootstrap profiles` (#69966, commit
`930852af29`) added `bootstrapMaxChars` / `bootstrapTotalMaxChars` /
`contextInjection` overrides under `agents.list[]` and threaded the resolved
session agent id through the embedded runner, CLI runner, compaction path,
and `/context` diagnostics. `noteBootstrapFileSize` in
`src/commands/doctor-bootstrap-size.ts` was missed in that thread-through, so
`openclaw doctor` still resolves only `agents.defaults.bootstrap*MaxChars`.

For users who configure a tighter or looser per-agent override, `openclaw
doctor` either over- or under-warns and the remediation tip points the user
at the wrong knob. The "Real behavior proof" section below shows three
scenarios producing identical wrong output on `origin/main` vs three
correctly differentiated outputs after this patch.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #69966 (introduced per-agent bootstrap profiles; this PR fills in
  the doctor call site missed by that thread-through)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: `openclaw doctor`'s `Bootstrap file size` note reports
percentages and remediation tips against `agents.defaults.bootstrap*MaxChars`
even when the default agent has a per-agent override set, so the reported
percent-of-max and tip wording do not match the budget the runtime actually
uses.

Real environment tested:
- OS: Windows 11 Enterprise 10.0.26200
- Runtime: Node v24.14.1, pnpm 11.1.0 (corepack-pinned at `package.json`)
- Workspace: temp dir with a 25 000-char `AGENTS.md` to force truncation
- Config: default agent `main`, `agents.defaults.bootstrapMaxChars = 20 000`
  (`Total = 100 000`), `agents.list[main].bootstrapMaxChars` set per scenario

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
# Reproducer that imports noteBootstrapFileSize directly and captures stdout
# (the same code path openclaw doctor executes; the doctor command calls
# noteBootstrapFileSize from src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts:573).
# Running it against this branch yields the AFTER output below; checking out
# main first yields the BEFORE output (also captured below for comparison).
node --import tsx repro.local.mjs
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
=== A: defaults-only (defaultsMax=20000) ===
◇  Bootstrap file size ─────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│  Workspace bootstrap files exceed limits and will be truncated:       │
│  - AGENTS.md: 25,000 raw / 19,999 injected (20% truncated; max/file)  │
│  Total bootstrap injected chars: 19,999 (20% of max/total 100,000).   │
│  Total bootstrap raw chars (before truncation): 25,000.               │
│  - Tip: tune `agents.list[].bootstrapMaxChars` for this agent, or     │
│    `agents.defaults.bootstrapMaxChars` as fallback, for per-file      │
│    limits.                                                            │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯

=== B: per-agent LARGER (perAgentMax=40000, defaultsMax=20000) ===
(no warning emitted)

=== C: per-agent SMALLER (perAgentMax=10000, defaultsMax=20000) ===
◇  Bootstrap file size ────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│  Workspace bootstrap files exceed limits and will be truncated:      │
│  - AGENTS.md: 25,000 raw / 9,999 injected (60% truncated; max/file)  │
│  Total bootstrap injected chars: 9,999 (10% of max/total 100,000).   │
│  Total bootstrap raw chars (before truncation): 25,000.              │
│  - Tip: tune `agents.list[].bootstrapMaxChars` for this agent, or    │
│    `agents.defaults.bootstrapMaxChars` as fallback, for per-file     │
│    limits.                                                           │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
```

Before-fix output (origin/main version of `doctor-bootstrap-size.ts`, same
reproducer, same temp workspace) — included for comparison:

```text
=== A: defaults-only (defaultsMax=20000) ===
◇  Bootstrap file size ──────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│  Workspace bootstrap files exceed limits and will be truncated:        │
│  - AGENTS.md: 25,000 raw / 19,999 injected (20% truncated; max/file)   │
│  Total bootstrap injected chars: 19,999 (20% of max/total 100,000).    │
│  Total bootstrap raw chars (before truncation): 25,000.                │
│  - Tip: tune `agents.defaults.bootstrapMaxChars` for per-file limits.  │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯

=== B: per-agent LARGER (perAgentMax=40000, defaultsMax=20000) ===
◇  Bootstrap file size ──────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│  Workspace bootstrap files exceed limits and will be truncated:        │
│  - AGENTS.md: 25,000 raw / 19,999 injected (20% truncated; max/file)   │
│  Total bootstrap injected chars: 19,999 (20% of max/total 100,000).    │
│  Total bootstrap raw chars (before truncation): 25,000.                │
│  - Tip: tune `agents.defaults.bootstrapMaxChars` for per-file limits.  │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯

=== C: per-agent SMALLER (perAgentMax=10000, defaultsMax=20000) ===
◇  Bootstrap file size ──────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│  Workspace bootstrap files exceed limits and will be truncated:        │
│  - AGENTS.md: 25,000 raw / 19,999 injected (20% truncated; max/file)   │
│  Total bootstrap injected chars: 19,999 (20% of max/total 100,000).    │
│  Total bootstrap raw chars (before truncation): 25,000.                │
│  - Tip: tune `agents.defaults.bootstrapMaxChars` for per-file limits.  │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
```

On `origin/main`, all three scenarios render the SAME warning (20% truncated
against the 20 000-char defaults budget) because the per-agent override is
silently dropped before reaching the resolver. After this patch, scenarios B
and C correctly reflect the per-agent override.

Observed result after fix:
- Scenario A is unchanged (defaults-only setup): same `20% / 20 000` numbers.
- Scenario B (per-agent budget LARGER than defaults) now stays silent — the
  file fits in the 40 000-char per-agent budget.
- Scenario C (per-agent budget SMALLER than defaults) now reports `60%
  truncated at 10 000` instead of `20% at 20 000`, matching the budget the
  runtime actually enforces.
- Both truncation tips now point at the per-agent knob first.

What was not tested:
- Non-default agents (out of scope — doctor still inspects only the default
  agent's workspace; covering additional agents would be a separate change).
- The heartbeat / cron `contextInjection` mode path (already covered by the
  existing `resolveContextInjectionMode` tests in `bootstrap-files.test.ts`).
- Live `openclaw doctor` CLI invocation against a populated state dir — the
  reproducer exercises the same `noteBootstrapFileSize` entry point that
  `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts:573` calls, with the same module
  graph, so the rendered output above is what `openclaw doctor` prints.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `noteBootstrapFileSize` was authored before #69966 and was
  not updated when that PR turned `resolveBootstrapMaxChars`,
  `resolveBootstrapTotalMaxChars`, and `resolveBootstrapContextForRun` into
  agent-aware resolvers. The other call sites in the embedded runner
  (`runEmbeddedAttempt`), CLI runner (`prepareCliRunContext`), compaction
  (`compactEmbeddedPiSessionDirectOnce`), and `/context` reporting were
  threaded; the doctor warning path was not.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the existing tests mocked the resolver
  functions but did not assert what arguments they were called with, so the
  regression passed silently.
- Contributing context: each resolver defaults `agentId` to `undefined`,
  which collapses cleanly to `agents.defaults.*` — the bug is silent rather
  than a runtime error, which is why it slipped through code review and
  CI on #69966.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/commands/doctor-bootstrap-size.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when `resolveDefaultAgentId` returns a
  specific agent id, `noteBootstrapFileSize` passes that same agent id into
  `resolveBootstrapMaxChars`, `resolveBootstrapTotalMaxChars`, and
  `resolveBootstrapContextForRun`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is purely a missed
  argument at the call site. Asserting call arguments catches it without
  needing fixtures for the resolver internals (which are already covered by
  `pi-embedded-helpers.buildbootstrapcontextfiles.test.ts` and
  `bootstrap-files.test.ts`).
- Existing test that already covers this: none — the existing tests asserted
  on the rendered warning string but did not pin the call arguments, so the
  same string was emitted regardless of whether `agentId` was forwarded.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — a new test (`threads the default
  agent id through bootstrap size resolution`) is added in this PR.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

For users who set `agents.list[].bootstrapMaxChars` or
`agents.list[].bootstrapTotalMaxChars` on the default agent:

- `openclaw doctor` percent-of-max numbers in the `Bootstrap file size`
  warning now reflect the per-agent override rather than the global default.
- The two remediation tips now read "Tip: tune
  `agents.list[].bootstrapMaxChars` for this agent, or
  `agents.defaults.bootstrapMaxChars` as fallback, for per-file limits."
  (previously mentioned only the defaults knob).

For users who only use `agents.defaults.*`: no behavior change (the per-agent
resolver falls back to defaults; see Scenario A in the proof section).

## Diagram

N/A — single call-site fix.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows 11 Enterprise (10.0.26200)
- Runtime: Node v24.14.1, pnpm 11.1.0 (corepack-pinned)
- Model/provider: N/A (doctor command path; no model invocation)
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default agent `main` with
  `agents.list[main].bootstrapMaxChars` set lower or higher than
  `agents.defaults.bootstrapMaxChars` to surface the discrepancy.

### Steps

1. Configure `agents.list[main].bootstrapMaxChars` differently from
   `agents.defaults.bootstrapMaxChars` in the OpenClaw config.
2. Place a bootstrap file (e.g. `AGENTS.md`) in the default agent's workspace
   sized to land between the two budgets (or above both).
3. Run `openclaw doctor` and inspect the `Bootstrap file size` note.

### Expected

Percent-of-max and tip text reflect `agents.list[main].bootstrap*MaxChars`
when the per-agent override exists, and `agents.defaults.*` otherwise.

### Actual (before this patch)

Percent-of-max and tip text always reflect `agents.defaults.bootstrap*MaxChars`,
silently ignoring the per-agent override (see Scenario B and C in the proof
section above).

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (`src/commands/doctor-bootstrap-size.test.ts`)
- [x] Trace/log snippets (rendered `Bootstrap file size` notes above)
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Local validation log:

```text
# format check
$ pnpm format:check src/commands/doctor-bootstrap-size.ts src/commands/doctor-bootstrap-size.test.ts
Checking formatting...
All matched files use the correct format.

# lint
$ pnpm exec oxlint --type-aware src/commands/doctor-bootstrap-size.ts src/commands/doctor-bootstrap-size.test.ts
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.

# typecheck
$ pnpm tsgo:core
(no errors)

# focused tests
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-bootstrap-size.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  3 passed (3)

# regression test fails on origin/main (without the .ts fix)
$ git checkout main -- src/commands/doctor-bootstrap-size.ts
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-bootstrap-size.test.ts
 Test Files  1 failed (1)
      Tests  2 failed | 1 passed (3)
# (asserts on resolveBootstrapMaxChars and resolveBootstrapContextForRun
# argument lists fail because the agent id is dropped before the call.)
```

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios:
  - Defaults-only configuration: behavior unchanged (Scenario A above).
  - Per-agent budget LARGER than defaults: doctor correctly stays silent
    (Scenario B above; on `main` it falsely warned).
  - Per-agent budget SMALLER than defaults: doctor correctly reports the
    tighter per-agent budget (Scenario C above; on `main` it reported
    against the looser defaults budget).
  - Regression test fails on `origin/main` and passes after the fix.
- Edge cases checked: `resolveDefaultAgentId` returning a non-`"main"` id
  (covered by the new unit test); per-agent override absent / present;
  near-limit vs hard truncation; truncation cause attribution (`per-file-limit`
  vs `total-limit`).
- What I did **not** verify:
  - Non-default agents in a multi-agent config — `noteBootstrapFileSize`
    inspects only the default agent's workspace, so covering other agents
    is a separate change.
  - Live `openclaw doctor` against a fully provisioned state dir — the
    reproducer above exercises the same entry point with the same module
    graph (`src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts:573` is the call site
    in the doctor flow), so the rendered output matches what `openclaw
    doctor` would print.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I will reply to or resolve every bot review conversation I address in this PR.
- [x] I will leave unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a third call site of `resolveBootstrapMaxChars`,
  `resolveBootstrapTotalMaxChars`, `resolveBootstrapContextForRun`, or
  `resolveContextInjectionMode` still drops the agent id.
  - Mitigation: a repo-wide grep for those four symbols confirms the doctor
    path (`src/commands/doctor-bootstrap-size.ts`) was the only remaining
    call site without an agent id after #69966. All other call sites in
    `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts`, `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts`,
    `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.ts`, and
    `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-context-report.ts` were threaded by #69966.
- Risk: tip text wording change breaks a downstream string assertion.
  - Mitigation: the existing assertion in `doctor-bootstrap-size.test.ts` was
    the only string-level assertion on the tip text in this repo (grep on
    "Tip: tune"); it is updated in this PR.

target.title: Doctor: expose extra gateway service findings
target.body:
## Summary

- Exposes the extra gateway service doctor checks as structured health findings.
- Adds dry-run repair effects for legacy gateway cleanup that doctor already knows how to describe.
- Keeps the existing `doctor:gateway-services` legacy run path authoritative for real normal `doctor` / `doctor --fix` behavior.
- Declares the structured checks inline on the existing ordered doctor contribution, so core doctor ordering remains contribution-owned.
- Preserves compatibility for intentional non-legacy extra gateway services by reporting them as informational instead of warning-level lint failures.

This is intentionally the gateway services slice from the doctor repair stack. It does not change gateway startup, provider config, or plugin health behavior.

## Stack

Stacked on #86627. Current branch head is signed commit `5d57cbeccc55509c3867bb4c964a0094c6869293` (`test(doctor): fix gateway CI expectations`).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Extra or legacy gateway-like services are visible through structured doctor health checks, while normal mutating service cleanup remains owned by the existing `doctor:gateway-services` run path. Intentional non-legacy extra gateway services are now non-failing by default; legacy gateway services remain warning-level and repairable.

Real environment tested:
WSL Ubuntu 24.04, OpenClaw checkout `/root/src/openclaw-84340-proof`, PR head `5d57cbeccc55509c3867bb4c964a0094c6869293`, source CLI via `node scripts/run-node.mjs`, source harness via `node --import tsx --input-type=module`, isolated temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temp `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, temp `HOME`, and a synthetic legacy user systemd unit.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Reused the prior isolated source CLI lint proof for a synthetic legacy `clawdbot-gateway.service` unit.
2. Ran a source harness importing `extraGatewayServiceToHealthFinding`, `extraGatewayServiceToRepairEffects`, and `exitCodeFromFindings`.
3. The harness compared an intentional non-legacy extra gateway service with a legacy gateway service under the default lint threshold.
4. Ran focused validation: `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts --reporter=dot`.
5. Ran changed-file checks: targeted `oxlint`, targeted `oxfmt --check`, `git diff --check`, and `pnpm tsgo:core`.
6. After the test-only CI follow-up, ran the failing CI coverage locally: `src/cli/program/register.maintenance.test.ts`, `src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts`, `pnpm check:test-types`, and `pnpm tsgo:core`.

Evidence after fix:
```json
{
  "intentional": {
    "severity": "info",
    "fixHint": "Run a single gateway per machine unless this extra gateway is intentional.",
    "defaultLintExit": 0,
    "repairEffects": []
  },
  "legacy": {
    "severity": "warning",
    "fixHint": "Run openclaw doctor --fix to remove legacy gateway services.",
    "defaultLintExit": 1,
    "repairEffects": [
      {
        "kind": "service",
        "action": "would-remove-legacy-gateway-service",
        "target": "openclaw-gateway.service",
        "dryRunSafe": false
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

The earlier real CLI proof still applies to the legacy path: `doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/gateway-services/extra` reports the synthetic legacy `clawdbot-gateway.service` as a structured warning with the expected check id, Linux source, target service label, and fix hint.

Observed result after fix:
Intentional non-legacy extra gateway services are visible in structured output without failing default `doctor --lint`. Legacy gateway services remain warning-level, fail default lint, and expose the dry-run cleanup effect.

What was not tested:
The proof uses synthetic gateway service fixtures, not a real installed or running Gateway service. I did not run full `doctor --fix` service cleanup in WSL because the mutating cleanup path can invoke systemd/service-management behavior; the PR intentionally keeps that existing legacy run path authoritative.

## Validation

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts --reporter=dot` -> 82 tests passed across 2 shards
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.ts src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts` -> 0 warnings, 0 errors
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.ts src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/program/register.maintenance.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts --reporter=dot` -> 26 tests passed across 2 shards
- `pnpm check:test-types`

target.title: fix(slack): forward per-agent identity overlay on heartbeat and runtimeSend (#84297)
target.body: ## Summary Per-agent Slack identity (`agents.list[].identity`) doesn't survive the heartbeat / announce path. Two things go wrong: 1. The overlay gets dropped before it reaches the channel adapter — both in the heartbeat runner and in the legacy CLI `runtimeSend` factory. 2. Even when identity *does* reach the Slack adapter, the helper that maps `OutboundIdentity.emoji` → `chat.postMessage.icon_emoji` only accepts `:shortcode:` form and silently throws away raw Unicode emoji like `📟` — which is exactly how most people configure it. So every heartbeat-driven message posts under the generic app bot instead of the configured persona ("Pulse 📟"). The reply path already handles this correctly (since #38235); the announce/heartbeat path was just never wired up. **The fix:** thread identity through the heartbeat runner and the `runtimeSend` factory to the adapter, and stop the Slack adapter from filtering raw Unicode emoji so the configured value actually reaches `icon_emoji`. **What changed:** - Added `RuntimeSendOpts.identity?: OutboundIdentity`, forwarded to every adapter entrypoint (`sendText` / `sendMedia` / `sendPayload`). - The heartbeat runner resolves the agent's identity once (`resolveAgentOutboundIdentity(cfg, agentId)`, right next to `outboundSession`) and passes it into both `sendDurableMessageBatch` calls. - `resolveSlackSendIdentity` in `extensions/slack/src/outbound-adapter.ts` now passes `identity.emoji` through untouched — the same thing the reply path already does in `src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.ts`. - Tests: unit tests for the `runtimeSend` factory, an end-to-end test that drives `runHeartbeatOnce` against a real Slack test plugin, and 4 adapter tests (raw Unicode, `:shortcode:`, `iconUrl`-wins, no-identity). **Proof status:** the live-Slack proof ClawSweeper asked for (`status: 📣 needs proof`) is now in hand — see **Real behavior proof** below. The reporter (@aw-stevens), who runs the affected `chat:write.customize` deployment, confirmed on the real workspace that Slack accepts the raw `📟` (`ok:true`, no fallback) and that the `username` override flips the message off the shared app identity onto the per-agent persona. **What this PR deliberately leaves alone:** the cron `--announce` path. On current `main` it already goes through `resolveAgentOutboundIdentity` (`src/cron/delivery.ts`, lines 68 / 105). The reporter's original trace was against the v2026.5.18 dist, where both cron and heartbeat still ran through the legacy factory; cron has since moved to `sendDurableMessageBatch` with identity wired up, but heartbeat kept the bug. The reply path (#38235), the missing-scope retry, and `icon_url`-wins-over-`icon_emoji` are all untouched. ## Motivation In a shared Slack channel, the per-agent identity overlay is the only thing that tells you *which* agent posted what. Without it, every cron digest and every heartbeat push from every agent shows up under one generic app bot name and icon — and operators are forced into ugly workarounds: prefixing every template with the agent name by hand, or giving each agent its own channel and losing the whole point of a shared channel. The reporter laid this out cleanly in #84297 with a source trace pointing straight at the gap in the runtime-send factory. ClawSweeper's review then caught a second, related problem: the Slack adapter's raw-Unicode emoji filter, which would have left the persona half-broken even after the threading fix. The reporter has since gone and captured the live `chat:write.customize` proof for both Slack-side unknowns (see **Real behavior proof**). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #84297 - Related #38235 (reply-path identity fix this PR complements) - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) Proven on both sides of the seam: OC threads `agents.list[].identity` into `chat.postMessage` (source-level plus the new tests), and Slack itself accepts and renders that exact call shape — the latter captured live by the reporter (@aw-stevens) on the real `chat:write.customize` workspace #84297 was filed from. The OC half is what our code controls; the Slack half is server behavior no source-level test can reach. Together they close the `status: 📣 needs proof` gate. - Behavior or issue addressed: per-agent Slack identity (`agents.list[].identity` → `name` / `emoji` / `avatarUrl`) is applied on heartbeat target-channel pushes and through the legacy CLI `runtimeSend` factory, and the raw Unicode emoji form (`📟`) survives end-to-end onto `chat.postMessage.icon_emoji`. - Real environment tested: two complementary setups. (1) OC side — from this machine (no live Slack bot here) I ran the patched runtime semantics directly under `node`: `createChannelOutboundRuntimeSend.buildContext()`, the heartbeat-runner identity wiring, and the Slack adapter's `resolveSlackSendIdentity`. (2) Slack side — the reporter ran a controlled `chat.postMessage` pair from the real shared bot token on the production `chat:write.customize` workspace, reproducing the exact call the patched adapter emits. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the verification script locally, then the reporter ran a live control/customized pair on the real workspace. ``` node verify-identity-fix.mjs ``` On the reporter's live workspace: a control `chat.postMessage` with no overrides, then a customized one (`username` = Pulse, `icon_emoji` = raw Unicode 📟) from the same bot token seconds apart into the same channel, followed by a `conversations.history` read-back. - Evidence after fix (terminal capture plus live Slack API responses): real stdout from the local run — ```text Verifying identity propagation through runtime-send + heartbeat-runner + Slack adapter (issue #84297) [1/6] runtime sendText receives identity (name=Pulse, emoji=📟) [2/6] runtime sendMedia receives identity (name=Pulse) [3/6] runtime omits identity when caller does not supply it (identity=undefined) [4/6] heartbeat-runner threads identity into both batches (name=Pulse, emoji=📟) [5/6] Slack adapter preserves raw Unicode emoji on icon_emoji (iconEmoji=📟) ← reviewer P2 [6/6] Slack adapter still accepts :shortcode: form for callers (iconEmoji=:beeper:) All 6 scenarios pass. Per-agent persona overlay reaches chat.postMessage with raw Unicode emoji preserved. ``` Live `chat:write.customize` scope, straight from the response header (no posting required): ``` x-oauth-scopes: chat:write, chat:write.customize, app_mentions:read, … (truncated) ``` `chat.postMessage` responses (post-call echo) — control vs. customized, same bot token: ```jsonc // (1) CONTROL — no override { "ok": true, "ts": "…820.7", "echo": { "bot_id": "B0…JG17S", "bot_profile_name": "DemandBots" // ← generic shared app identity } } // (2) CUSTOMIZED — username + raw-Unicode icon_emoji { "ok": true, "ts": "…821.4", "echo": { "username": "Pulse", // ← per-agent name overrides "DemandBots" "icons": { "emoji": "📟" }, // ← RAW UNICODE accepted & applied, ok:true "bot_id": "B0…JG17S" // ← SAME token as the control } } ``` `conversations.history` read-back (authoritative persisted render): ```jsonc [ { "ts": "…821.4", "subtype": "bot_message", "username": "Pulse", "icons": { "emoji": "📟" }, "bot_id": "B0…JG17S" }, // customized { "ts": "…820.7", "bot_profile": { "name": "DemandBots" }, "bot_id": "B0…JG17S" } // control ] ``` Rendered Slack bubbles — the control posts under the generic "DemandBots" app, the customized one posts under "Pulse 📟": - Observed result after fix: `RuntimeSendOpts.identity` reaches `ChannelOutboundContext.identity` on every send shape; `runHeartbeatOnce` resolves identity once and threads it into both `sendDurableMessageBatch` calls (heartbeat-ok and main reply); `resolveSlackSendIdentity` hands the raw emoji straight to `chat.postMessage`. Live, Slack accepted the raw 📟 (`ok:true`, persisted as `icons.emoji` in `conversations.history`, no fallback and no `:emoji_not_found:`), and the `username` override rendered the per-agent persona ("Pulse") distinct from the shared "DemandBots" identity — same token, seconds apart. That clears the P2 / `merge-risk: message-delivery` concern: Slack does not reject raw Unicode here, so the passthrough needs no normalization. The missing-scope fallback is unchanged, so workspaces without `chat:write.customize` still degrade to the app identity. - What was not tested: a fully OC-generated heartbeat fire (`runHeartbeatOnce` or the scheduler) into a live workspace, as opposed to the controlled `chat.postMessage` that emits the same call shape. The reporter's `auth.test` curl in #84297 confirms scope is granted on the affected deployment, so the OC wiring is the load-bearing change; a maintainer can run the final visual check with `@openclaw-mantis slack desktop smoke: verify an agent heartbeat to Slack with identity name and emoji renders under that persona.` - Before evidence: on unpatched `main` (2026.5.26), the 8:07 AM heartbeat/cron announce lands under the generic "DemandBots" app, while the 4:48 PM @-mention reply renders correctly aliased (the post-#38235 reply path) — so the announce/heartbeat path is the gap. The #84297 write-up (evidence A/B/C) documents the v2026.5.18 behavior: scope granted, `buildContext()` dropping `opts.identity`, and the delivery mirror showing zero `username` / `icon_emoji` / `icon_url` references. ## Root Cause Three layered gaps: 1. **The heartbeat runner never forwarded identity.** Both `sendDurableMessageBatch` calls (heartbeat-ok around L1701, main reply around L1981) passed `session: outboundSession` and `deps: opts.deps`, but no `identity`. 2. **The `runtimeSend` factory dropped it too.** `buildContext()` in `channel-outbound-send.ts` didn't carry `opts.identity` through on the legacy CLI path. 3. **The Slack adapter filtered out raw Unicode.** Even with identity threaded through, `resolveSlackSendIdentity` ran `emoji` through `/^:[^:\s]+:$/` and turned anything that wasn't `:shortcode:` into `undefined` — so `identity.emoji: "📟"` got stripped before it ever reached `icon_emoji`. Why nothing caught it: there was no test checking that resolved identity reaches the adapter on the announce/heartbeat path, and nothing exercising the adapter with raw Unicode. #38235 wired up the reply path, but only covered the streaming reply context — the heartbeat path builds its own send-batch shape that fix never touched, and the emoji filter was never tested against the raw input people actually use. Context worth knowing: cron `--announce` moved off the legacy factory onto `sendDurableMessageBatch` between v2026.5.18 and `main`, and happened to thread identity correctly on the way. Heartbeat went through the same migration but kept the bug. That's why the reporter's v2026.5.18 trace showed both broken, while on `main` cron is fine and heartbeat isn't — and the emoji filter has been quietly degrading every caller (cron included) that uses a raw-Unicode emoji. ## Regression Test Plan - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - **Where:** `src/cli/send-runtime/channel-outbound-send.test.ts` (4 new cases), `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.identity.test.ts` (new file, 2 cases driving `runHeartbeatOnce` against a real Slack test plugin), and `extensions/slack/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts` (4 cases: raw Unicode, `:shortcode:`, `iconUrl`-wins, no-identity). - **What they lock in:** when `agents.list[].identity` is set, the resolved `OutboundIdentity` reaches the adapter on every `runtimeSend` shape (`sendText` / `sendMedia` / `sendPayload`); the heartbeat runner threads it into the Slack send dep on both the heartbeat-ok and main reply paths; and `resolveSlackSendIdentity` keeps the configured emoji verbatim, raw (`📟`) or shortcode (`:beeper:`). With no identity configured, the field stays `undefined`, so nothing changes for existing callers. - **Why this is enough:** the bug lives in exactly three places — the factory's `buildContext()`, the heartbeat runner's two batch calls, and the adapter's emoji mapping — and all three now have focused coverage that needs no live workspace. The integration test runs the real `runHeartbeatOnce` through the existing `heartbeat-runner.test-harness` Slack plugin, so a regression at any layer fails a test. - **Existing coverage:** none for the announce/heartbeat path or the raw-emoji mapping. `send.identity-fallback.test.ts` covers the missing-scope retry, but only with `:shortcode:` form, which never tripped the filter. - If no new test is added: N/A — new tests are added. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Heartbeat-driven Slack messages now render under the configured per-agent persona on any workspace that grants `chat:write.customize` — matching what the reply path has done since #38235. `agents.list[].identity.emoji` can now be a raw Unicode emoji (`"📟"`) and it's applied verbatim to `chat.postMessage.icon_emoji`; previously this adapter path only honored `:shortcode:`. Workspaces without `chat:write.customize` keep falling back to the app identity through the existing retry, exactly as before. Other channels with identity-overlay support (Discord webhook routing, Feishu cards) pick up the factory fix for free if they hit that path. No new config and no new flags — this just honors the `agents.list[].identity` you already have, on paths that used to drop or filter it. ## Diagram How the heartbeat path and the adapter behave before vs. after: ```text Before (heartbeat path): [heartbeat tick] -> resolveDeliveryTarget -> outboundSession -> sendDurableMessageBatch({ session, deps }) ^ identity dropped here -> Slack adapter sees ctx.identity = undefined -> chat.postMessage with no username/icon overrides -> message renders under generic app identity Before (Slack adapter, even when identity does arrive): ctx.identity.emoji = "RAW UNICODE" -> resolveSlackSendIdentity() -> /^:[^:\s]+:$/.test(raw unicode) === false -> iconEmoji = undefined (raw Unicode silently dropped) -> chat.postMessage with no icon_emoji -> message renders under generic app identity After (heartbeat path): [heartbeat tick] -> resolveDeliveryTarget -> outboundSession -> resolveAgentOutboundIdentity(cfg, agentId) -> sendDurableMessageBatch({ session, identity, deps }) -> Slack adapter resolveSlackSendIdentity(ctx.identity) -> chat.postMessage with username + icon_emoji -> message renders under "Pulse" After (Slack adapter): ctx.identity.emoji = "RAW UNICODE" (or ":beeper:") -> resolveSlackSendIdentity() -> iconEmoji = ctx.identity.emoji (raw value passed through) -> chat.postMessage with icon_emoji = the configured value -> message renders under the configured persona Legacy runtimeSend factory (channel-outbound-send.ts): Before: buildContext() -> { cfg, to, text, ... gatewayClientScopes } (identity silently dropped) After: buildContext() -> { cfg, to, text, ... gatewayClientScopes, identity } ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No — it's the same `chat.postMessage` call, just with `username` / `icon_emoji` / `icon_url` filled in from existing config when the agent has an identity overlay. - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No - If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - Channel: Slack via `@openclaw/slack`, socket mode. The bot scope needs `chat:write.customize` for the overlay to take effect server-side. Heartbeat set to `target: "slack"`, `to: "channel:C0XXXXXXX"`. - Config (redacted): ```yaml agents: list: - id: pulse identity: name: Pulse emoji: 📟 heartbeat: every: 2h target: slack to: channel:C0XXXXXXX accountId: default ``` ### Steps 1. Fire a heartbeat tick for an agent with `identity` configured (`runHeartbeatOnce`, or wait for the scheduler) targeting a Slack channel. 2. Watch the message land in the channel. 3. Before this patch it shows up under the generic app bot + default icon. After, it shows up under "Pulse 📟" on any `chat:write.customize` workspace — whether the emoji is raw Unicode or `:shortcode:`. ### Expected The heartbeat message shows the per-agent `name` and the configured `emoji` from `agents.list[].identity`, just like the reply path does post-#38235. ### Actual With the patch, the heartbeat runner resolves the agent's identity once next to `outboundSession` and forwards it into both `sendDurableMessageBatch` calls, the `runtimeSend` factory now forwards `identity` too, and `resolveSlackSendIdentity` keeps the raw emoji so `chat.postMessage.icon_emoji` carries it through. The reporter's live diagnostic (**Real behavior proof**) confirms Slack accepts the raw `icon_emoji` and renders the per-agent `username` for that exact call. ## Human Verification (required) Verified: - `RuntimeSendOpts.identity` reaches `ChannelOutboundContext.identity` for `sendText`, `sendMedia`, and `sendPayload` (block) sends. - `runtimeSend.sendMessage(...)` leaves `identity` undefined when the caller doesn't pass it (no change for unconfigured callers). - `runHeartbeatOnce` resolves the agent's identity from `agents.list[].identity` and threads it into the third arg of the Slack send dep used by the heartbeat test plugin. - With no identity configured, the heartbeat send leaves `identity` undefined. - `resolveSlackSendIdentity` keeps raw Unicode emoji (`📟`) on `iconEmoji`. - `resolveSlackSendIdentity` still accepts `:shortcode:` form (`":beeper:"`). - `iconUrl` wins over `iconEmoji` when both are set (Slack treats them as exclusive). - Cron `--announce` already passes identity on `main` (`src/cron/delivery.ts`, lines 68 / 105) — confirmed by reading the path; no new wiring needed. - **Live, by @aw-stevens on the real `chat:write.customize` workspace:** Slack accepts raw `📟` with `ok:true` and persists it in `conversations.history` (no fallback, no `:emoji_not_found:`), and the `username` overlay renders "Pulse" distinct from the shared "DemandBots" identity — same token, seconds apart. Edge cases: agent with `name` only / `emoji` only / both / neither (`resolveAgentOutboundIdentity` returns `undefined` when all-empty, so the field stays undefined). The heartbeat-ok and main reply sends carry the same resolved identity. Discord webhook and Feishu cards inherit the factory fix if they reach it. Workspaces without `chat:write.customize` hit the existing missing-scope retry — unchanged. What I couldn't do myself: a live `chat:write.customize` run from this machine (no spare bot/workspace). The reporter's diagnostic covers the Slack acceptance and render half; the only step nobody's run is a fully OC-generated heartbeat into a live workspace (vs. the controlled `chat.postMessage` of the same shape). Their `auth.test` curl in #84297 confirms scope is granted on the affected deployment, so the OC wiring is the load-bearing part — and a maintainer can do the final visual check with `@openclaw-mantis slack desktop smoke: verify an agent heartbeat to Slack with identity name and emoji renders under that persona.` ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes. `RuntimeSendOpts.identity` is optional — callers that don't set it (or agents without an `identity` config) get exactly the old behavior. Callers already passing `:shortcode:` form to `resolveSlackSendIdentity` keep working unchanged, and the missing-scope retry still covers workspaces without `chat:write.customize`. - Config/env changes? None. `agents.list[].identity` already exists and was already read by other paths (`resolveAckReaction`, `resolveAssistantIdentity`, the reply path post-#38235) — this PR just stops dropping it on the announce/heartbeat path and stops filtering raw Unicode at the adapter. - Migration needed? No. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. ## Risks and Mitigations - **A workspace without `chat:write.customize` will ignore the new `username` / `icon_emoji` / `icon_url` fields** and keep showing the generic app identity. - This is Slack server behavior, not new here — those workspaces were never going to render the persona anyway. The reply path (post-#38235) has the same constraint, and `sendMessageSlack` already retries without custom identity when it hits a `chat:write.customize` scope error. - **A misconfigured `identity.emoji` with arbitrary text (e.g. `"hello world"`) now reaches `icon_emoji`** instead of being silently dropped by the old shortcode regex, so Slack may show an `:emoji_not_found:` placeholder. - The field is documented as an emoji and it's on the configurer to give a valid one. The reporter's live test confirms a valid raw emoji is accepted (`ok:true`); the failure mode for a bad value is a cosmetic "icon doesn't render," not a delivery error — the message body still posts. Same forgiveness as the reply path, which already passes the raw value through. - **The heartbeat runner resolves identity once per tick**, so in theory a config hot-reload mid-tick could give the heartbeat-ok and main reply different snapshots. - It's resolved once at the top of `runHeartbeatOnce` and reused for both calls in the same tick, so they always match. The next tick picks up any reloaded config naturally. - **A downstream caller of `runtimeSend.sendMessage` that relied on `buildContext()` omitting `identity`** could now read a value where it didn't before. - The field is only populated when the caller passes `opts.identity`, and no production caller on `main` does that today (cron routes through `sendDurableMessageBatch`, and heartbeat now does too). The remaining `runtimeSend` callers are CLI debug tools and extension paths that hand the context straight to adapters, which already handled the missing-identity case.

target.title: fix(gateway): mark SIGUSR1 token consumed on restartIntent path, reset stale tokens on in-process restart
target.body:
## Summary

`update.run` from the control UI downloads the new package but the gateway never restarts to pick it up. Every attempt logs `restart coalesced (already in-flight)` and is silently dropped for the lifetime of the process.

### Root cause

Two bugs combine to make the state permanent:

**Bug 1 — `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` (`onSigusr1`):**
When a `restartIntent` file is present, the handler calls `request()` and returns **without calling `markGatewaySigusr1RestartHandled()`**. This leaves `emittedRestartToken > consumedRestartToken`. Every subsequent `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart()` call hits the `hasUnconsumedRestartSignal()` guard and is dropped.

**Bug 2 — `src/infra/restart.ts` (`resetGatewayRestartStateForInProcessRestart`):**
This function clears timers and deferral polls but never aligns the token counters. A stale unconsumed token from the previous lifecycle carries forward into the next server iteration after an in-process restart.

### Fix

1. Call `markGatewaySigusr1RestartHandled()` before the `restartIntent` early-return in `onSigusr1` so the token is consumed regardless of which restart path is taken.
2. Align `consumedRestartToken = emittedRestartToken` in `resetGatewayRestartStateForInProcessRestart()` as a safety net so a stale token cannot survive an in-process restart boundary.

### Reproduction

1. Run the gateway as a long-lived systemd user service
2. Let a new version become available and trigger `update.run` (UI or CLI)
3. Observe: npm package updates on disk but gateway PID never changes
4. Every retry logs `restart coalesced (already in-flight)`

### Workaround (until patched)

```bash
systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway
```

### Real behavior log

**Platform:** systemd user service (`openclaw-gateway.service`), Ubuntu 24.04 LTS / WSL2

Two `update.run` attempts 11 minutes apart. `changedPaths=<n/a>` on the second attempt confirms the npm package updated successfully on attempt 1; only the restart was broken. Gateway process `<pid-a>` never changed between attempts.

```
# Attempt 1 — 15:25:47
<hostname> node[<pid-a>]: [restart] request coalesced (already in-flight) reason=update.run actor=openclaw-control-ui device=<device-id> ip=unknown-ip
<hostname> node[<pid-a>]: [gateway] update.run completed actor=openclaw-control-ui device=<device-id> ip=unknown-ip conn=<conn-uuid> changedPaths=<n/a> restartReason=update.run status=ok
<hostname> node[<pid-a>]: [gateway] update.run restart coalesced actor=openclaw-control-ui device=<device-id> ip=unknown-ip conn=<conn-uuid> delayMs=0

# Attempt 2 — 11 minutes later, same stuck process
<hostname> node[<pid-a>]: [restart] request coalesced (already in-flight) reason=update.run actor=openclaw-control-ui device=<device-id> ip=unknown-ip
<hostname> node[<pid-a>]: [gateway] update.run completed actor=openclaw-control-ui device=<device-id> ip=unknown-ip conn=<conn-uuid> changedPaths=<n/a> restartReason=update.run status=ok
<hostname> node[<pid-a>]: [gateway] update.run restart coalesced actor=openclaw-control-ui device=<device-id> ip=unknown-ip conn=<conn-uuid> delayMs=0

# Workaround: systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway
<hostname> node[<pid-a>]: [gateway] signal SIGTERM received
<hostname> node[<pid-a>]: [gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down
<hostname> node[<pid-a>]: [gateway] shutdown error: Error [ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND]: Cannot find module '/home/<user>/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/hook-runner-global-DIpzvALt.js' imported from /home/<user>/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/server.impl-D3CWr7f5.js
# hook-runner-global-DIpzvALt.js is a hashed filename from the new package version. The old process was holding stale module refs because npm swapped the package on disk but the process never restarted.

<hostname> systemd: Stopped openclaw-gateway.service
<hostname> systemd: Started openclaw-gateway.service
<hostname> node[<pid-b>]: [gateway] loading configuration...
<hostname> node[<pid-b>]: [gateway] resolving authentication...
<hostname> node[<pid-b>]: [gateway] starting HTTP server...
<hostname> node[<pid-b>]: [gateway] ready
# New PID, updated binary loaded successfully
```

### Regression tests

Two focused tests cover the two fixes and are included in this PR:

- `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`: "marks the in-flight token consumed before routing SIGUSR1 with a restart intent" asserts that `markGatewaySigusr1RestartHandled` is called before `request()` is routed in the `restartIntent` branch. Fails on current main, passes with the fix.
- `src/infra/infra-runtime.test.ts`: "aligns stale emit/consume token counters on in-process restart boundary" asserts that `resetGatewayRestartStateForInProcessRestart()` clears a stale unconsumed token so subsequent restart requests are not coalesced. Fails on current main, passes with the fix.

### Post-patch note

Post-patch behavior is validated by the regression tests added in this PR; both fail against current main and pass with the patch applied. End-to-end log proof of `update.run` succeeding post-patch is omitted because reproducing it requires cutting a new release for the patched gateway to pull from, which is outside contributor scope.

target.title: fix(discord): avoid duplicate typing keepalive for tool replies
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: message-tool-only Discord replies could refresh typing from two lifecycle owners, making Discord show stale multi-second "bot is typing" after the visible reply was already delivered.
- Solution: make the core typing keepalive an explicit per-run option, have Discord opt out only for unconfigured `message_tool_only` runs, and disable the Discord plugin's nested callback keepalive.
- What changed: added `typingKeepalive?: boolean` to reply options, kept shared reply typing periodic by default, pass `typingKeepalive: false` from Discord only when `sourceReplyDeliveryMode === "message_tool_only"` and no explicit `typingMode` is configured, and set the Discord channel callback keepalive interval to `0`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): explicit `agents.defaults.typingMode` / `session.typingMode` continues to win, non-Discord channels keep the existing shared keepalive default unless they opt out, typing is not disabled by default, ack reactions are unchanged, and this does not add a Discord "stop typing" call because Discord does not expose one.

## Motivation

- This tightens the user-visible lifecycle after the duplicate-reply regression was fixed. The run itself was clean, but Discord could continue to show typing because OpenClaw refreshed typing near or after the visible `message` tool delivery. That made it look as if a second response was still being generated even when the transcript showed no fallback or second assistant run.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #84059
- Related #84250
- Related #76091
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: stale Discord typing after a successful message-tool-only visible reply.
- Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw v2026.5.18 gateway, Node v25.6.1, Discord channel `#founders-club` (channel id ending `5258`), Pi runtime, `openai/gpt-5.5`, `messages.groupChat.visibleReplies: "message_tool"`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Applied the typing lifecycle patch locally to the installed OpenClaw runtime.
  2. Restarted the gateway.
  3. Sent Discord test messages in `#founders-club`.
  4. Checked the transcript and gateway log for second runs/fallbacks and observed Discord typing behavior.
- Evidence after fix (redacted runtime facts):
  - User message id `1506376771032056000`.
  - One `gpt-5.5` tool call at `2026-05-19T19:23:44.803Z`.
  - One delivery mirror at `2026-05-19T19:23:45.823Z`.
  - One successful `message` tool result at `2026-05-19T19:23:45.982Z` with primary Discord message id `1506376839398952981`.
  - Gateway log after delivery only showed context maintenance / normal bookkeeping; there was no second assistant run or fallback.
- Observed result after fix: typing is sent for the actual run, but OpenClaw no longer owns two periodic keepalive loops for the same Discord reply path. Residual short Discord-client typing visibility can still happen after the last typing pulse because Discord has no explicit stop-typing endpoint.
- What was not tested: full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`; I ran focused validation for the touched auto-reply and Discord extension surfaces.
- Before evidence: after the duplicate-reply fix, live Discord still showed "DrewBot is typing" after the visible reply even when logs showed no second assistant/fallback. That pointed at typing lifecycle refresh, not duplicate generation.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: default message-tool-only group replies start typing immediately, and Discord typing was being refreshed by both core reply typing and a Discord channel callback keepalive. For a path where the visible reply is delivered by the `message` tool, a late refresh can outlive the actual response and appear as phantom typing.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered typing start/cleanup behavior generally, but not "send the first typing signal without a periodic keepalive" or "Discord channel callbacks should not start a nested typing keepalive."
- Contributing context (if known): Discord typing indicators are TTL-based and cannot be explicitly stopped by the bot API.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: a typing controller can emit the first typing signal without periodic keepalive ticks; Discord processing does not start a nested callback keepalive; configured Discord `typingMode` preserves the core keepalive path.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it verifies the exact lifecycle knobs that caused stale typing without relying on Discord client TTL timing.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- For Discord message-tool-only source replies with no explicit `typingMode`, OpenClaw still sends the initial typing cue but does not periodically refresh it. Explicitly configured typing behavior and non-Discord shared defaults are preserved.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Discord source reply run -> core typing keepalive
                         -> Discord callback keepalive
                         -> visible message delivered -> typing may still be refreshed

After:
Discord source reply run -> core one-shot typing only for unconfigured message_tool_only
                         -> visible message delivered -> no OpenClaw periodic refresh by default
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw gateway, Node v25.6.1
- Model/provider: `openai/gpt-5.5`, Pi runtime
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord group/channel, message-tool-only visible replies
- Relevant config (redacted): `messages.groupChat.visibleReplies: "message_tool"`, no explicit `typingMode`

### Steps

1. Configure a Discord group/channel room for message-tool-only visible replies.
2. Trigger the agent in a group channel.
3. Have the assistant send the visible source reply via `message(action=send)`.
4. Observe Discord typing and inspect logs/transcript for whether a second run exists.

### Expected

- Typing starts for the real run.
- Visible reply sends once.
- OpenClaw does not continue periodically refreshing Discord typing after the reply path completes unless typing mode was explicitly configured.

### Actual

- Before this patch: stale typing could persist after the visible reply because multiple keepalive paths refreshed the indicator.
- After this patch: no duplicate keepalive path is active; live transcript/logs show one tool call, one delivery, one successful result, and no second run.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Focused validation run on this branch:

```text
git diff --check origin/main...HEAD
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/typing-persistence.test.ts
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
  Tests       73 passed (73)

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-discord.config.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests       71 passed (71)
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: live Discord message-tool-only reply after local runtime patch; transcript/logs showed one assistant tool call, one delivery mirror, one successful tool result, and no second assistant/fallback.
- Edge cases checked: explicit typing mode still preserves configured behavior; the one-shot behavior applies only to Discord when `sourceReplyDeliveryMode === "message_tool_only"` and typing mode is not configured.
- What you did **not** verify: full repository test suite, exact Discord client TTL duration after the final typing pulse.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Discord users who relied on unconfigured message-tool-only typing refreshing throughout very long runs may see fewer typing refreshes.
  - Mitigation: explicit `agents.defaults.typingMode` or `session.typingMode` continues to opt into configured behavior; non-Discord channels keep their existing shared keepalive default unless they explicitly opt out.

commands.review_summary:
The PR adds manual-run isolation to cron result handling, persists `lastRunWasManual`, updates the gateway cron schema, and expands cron regression coverage.

PR surface: Source +65, Tests +851. Total +916 across 9 files.

Reproducibility: yes. for the original bug at source level: current main and `v2026.6.9` route manual completion through the same success-only `deleteAfterRun` gate. I did not run a live gateway repro in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Human follow-up is needed because the branch still has a P1 cron state defect and the external contributor must refresh real behavior proof after repair.

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun and preserve counters for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw cron run` (manual/debug runs) on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes the job on success — consuming the one-shot and preventing its scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters, interfering with scheduled retry/backoff state.

This PR adds an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult` so manual runs preserve the job and leave scheduled execution state untouched.

## Root cause

`applyJobResult()` has no concept of manual vs scheduled runs. When a manual run succeeds on a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job, `shouldDelete` evaluates to `true` and the job is removed from the store — the scheduled fire never happens.

Additionally, manual error/skipped/success outcomes unconditionally mutate `consecutiveErrors` and `consecutiveSkipped` counters, which control retry backoff and auto-disable behavior for scheduled runs.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `timer.ts` | Add `isManual` option to `applyJobResult`; skip `deleteAfterRun` for manual runs; guard all counter mutations and alert emissions with `!isManual` |
| `ops.ts` | Pass `isManual: true` from manual run completion and invalid-persisted-run handling |
| `timer.regression.test.ts` | 5 new tests: manual ok/error preserve job, manual doesn't increment/reset counters |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts
 ✓ cron (45 tests) 144ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes it, preventing scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate scheduled retry counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `aa92a4a5b0`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.

### Source-level proof

```
Main branch applyJobResult():
  - shouldDelete = kind === "at" && deleteAfterRun && status === "ok"
    → no manual discriminator, always deletes on success
  - consecutiveErrors incremented unconditionally on error
  - consecutiveSkipped incremented unconditionally on skip

Fix branch:
  - shouldDelete = wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual
    → manual runs never trigger deletion
  - Counter mutations guarded: if (!opts?.isManual) { ... }
    → manual runs don't affect retry/backoff state
```

### Regression test coverage

```
✅ manual ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted
✅ manual error on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted  
✅ scheduled ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job deleted (unchanged behavior)
✅ manual error does not increment consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
✅ manual success does not reset consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
```

- **What was not tested**: Live `openclaw cron run` CLI flow (requires a running gateway with a configured `deleteAfterRun` at-job). The `isManual` flag propagation from `ops.ts` to `applyJobResult` is validated at the unit level.

Closes #83538


### Re-verification (2026-05-20)

Local checkout at `d89f14de26`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

 ✓ cron  ../../src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (39 tests) 116ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  39 passed (39)
   Duration  152ms
```

Full cron suite (`src/cron/`) also run: 94 test files, 901 passed. The 7 failures are in unrelated files (`run-log.test.ts`, `service.failure-alert.test.ts`) pre-existing on upstream/main.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `openclaw cron run` (manual) of a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job incorrectly deletes the job. Manual runs also pollute `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched checkout at `0cec2acc7a`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On `upstream/main`, counter mutations and `shouldDelete` run unconditionally — no `isManual` discriminator exists.
- **Exact steps after patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` + `src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 46/46 timer regression tests pass (including 7 new #83538/#83933-specific tests). 7/7 failure-alert tests pass.
- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI flow; the tests exercise the same `applyJobResult` code path the Gateway calls internally.

### Key test results

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun + success | NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun + success | Deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |
| Manual error → failure alert | NOT triggered ✅ |
| Scheduled error → failure alert | Triggered ✅ |


## Real behavior proof (Docker, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker container, `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched source at `885d605e56`.

### Latest fixes

| Issue | Problem | Fix |
|-------|---------|-----|
| P1 | Manual at-job error was disabling job (`enabled=false`, `nextRunAtMs=undefined`) | At-job error retry/disable block now guarded with `if (opts?.isManual)` |
| P2 | Manual success was clearing `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown | Cooldown clearing now inside `if (!opts?.isManual)` guard |

### Test results

| Test Suite | Result |
|------------|--------|
| Timer regression (49 tests) | ✅ All passed |
| Failure-alert (7 tests) | ✅ All passed |

### Key verified behaviors

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual at-job error | `enabled` stays true, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅ |
| Manual success | `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown preserved ✅ |
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun | Job NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun | Job deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |


## Real CLI proof (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway at `885d605e56` running with `--allow-unconfigured`.

**Terminal transcript**: `openclaw cron add` → `cron show` → `cron run` (manual, triggers error) → `cron show` (job still exists, enabled=true, nextRunAtMs preserved) → `cron list` (job present) → `cron runs` (run history with error status, nextRunAtMs unchanged).

| Step | Command | Result |
|------|---------|--------|
| Add job | `openclaw cron add --name proof-job --at ... --delete-after-run` | Created with `deleteAfterRun: true` |
| Manual run | `openclaw cron run <id>` | `{"ok": true, "enqueued": true}` |
| After run | `openclaw cron show <id>` | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` ✅ |
| Job exists | `openclaw cron list` | Job still listed ✅ |
| Run history | `openclaw cron runs --id <id>` | `status: "error"`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` ✅ |


## Real CLI proof at head SHA `642c3450d9` (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway built from PR head.

| Test | Command | Before (main) | After (PR head) |
|------|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| A: at-job deleteAfterRun | `cron run` → `cron show` | Job deleted/disabled | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅ |
| B: every-job manual error | `cron run` → `cron show` | `nextRunAtMs` rewritten by backoff | `nextRunAtMs` unchanged ✅ |
| A+B: counters | `cron runs` | `consecutiveErrors` mutated | Unchanged ✅ |

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` no longer consumes `deleteAfterRun` one-shot jobs, and no longer perturbs retry counters, at-job retry/disable state, recurring backoff, or failure-alert cooldown.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container built from PR HEAD `3d32c9baff`, Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.14.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `applyJobResult` has no manual-run discriminator — manual runs unconditionally drive `deleteAfterRun`, counter mutations, at-job disable, recurring backoff, and alert cooldown. The 12 regression tests added by this PR do not exist on main.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Build Docker image from PR HEAD
docker build --target build -t openclaw-pr83933:test .

# Test 1: Timer regression tests (includes all 12 manual-run isolation tests)
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

# Test 2: Failure alert tests (adapted for manual-run isolation)
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts

# Test 3: Lint check
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test npx oxlint src/cron/service/timer.ts src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts src/cron/service/ops.ts

# Test 4: Full cron test suite
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

Test 1 — Timer regression: **55 tests passed** (284ms)
```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (55 tests) 284ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  55 passed (55)
```

Test 2 — Failure alert: **10 tests passed** (63ms)
```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (10 tests) 63ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  10 passed (10)
```

Test 3 — Lint: **0 errors, 0 warnings**
```
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
Finished in 13ms on 4 files with 189 rules using 10 threads.
```

Test 4 — Full cron suite: **1000 tests passed** across 98 test files
```
 Test Files  1 failed | 97 passed (98)
      Tests  2 failed | 1000 passed (1002)
```
The 2 failures are in `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts` — these look for Swift source files (`apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/CronModels.swift`) which are not present in the Docker build target. These failures are pre-existing and unrelated to this PR.

- **Observed result after fix**: All 12 new regression tests pass, confirming that manual runs preserve one-shot jobs (`deleteAfterRun`), do not increment/reset counters, do not disable at-jobs on error, do not rewrite recurring `nextRunAtMs` with backoff, and do not clear failure-alert cooldown. Control-group tests confirm scheduled runs still drive all 5 state effects normally.

- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI `openclaw cron run` end-to-end (covered by earlier Docker CLI proof at `642c3450d9` — subsequent commits were formatting-only changes via oxfmt). The `cron-protocol-conformance` tests require macOS app source and cannot run in Docker.


## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `6bc3b9e374`, 2026-05-30)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` no longer consumes `deleteAfterRun` one-shot jobs, and no longer perturbs retry counters, at-job retry/disable state, recurring backoff, failure-alert cooldown, or skipped-run alert counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container built from PR HEAD `6bc3b9e374`, Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.14.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `applyJobResult` has no manual-run discriminator — manual runs unconditionally drive all 5 scheduled-state effects plus skipped-run alert paths. The 14 regression tests added by this PR do not exist on main.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
# Build Docker image from PR HEAD
docker build --target build -t openclaw-pr83933:test .

# Test 1: Timer regression tests (all 14 manual-run isolation tests)
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

# Test 2: Failure alert tests (adapted for manual-run isolation)
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts

# Test 3: Lint check
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test npx oxlint src/cron/service/timer.ts src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts src/cron/service/ops.ts

# Test 4: Full cron test suite
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

Test 1 — Timer regression: **56 tests passed** (234ms)
```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (56 tests) 234ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  56 passed (56)
```

Test 2 — Failure alert: **11 tests passed** (92ms)
```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (11 tests) 92ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  11 passed (11)
```

Test 3 — Lint: **0 errors, 0 warnings**
```
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
Finished in 10ms on 4 files with 189 rules using 10 threads.
```

Test 4 — Full cron suite: **1002 tests passed** across 98 test files
```
 Test Files  1 failed | 97 passed (98)
      Tests  2 failed | 1002 passed (1004)
```
The 2 failures are in `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts` — these look for Swift source files (`apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/CronModels.swift`) which are not present in the Docker build target. Pre-existing and unrelated to this PR.

- **Observed result after fix**: All 14 new regression tests pass, covering all 6 guarded state effects including the newly-added manual skipped-run isolation:
  - Manual `deleteAfterRun` at-job → NOT deleted ✅
  - Manual error → counters NOT incremented ✅
  - Manual success → counters NOT reset, cooldown NOT cleared ✅
  - Manual at-job error → NOT disabled, nextRunAtMs preserved ✅
  - Manual every-job error → backoff NOT applied, nextRunAtMs preserved ✅
  - Manual skipped at-job → consecutiveSkipped NOT incremented, alert NOT fired, all state preserved ✅
  - Manual skipped run with `includeSkipped: true` → failure alert NOT emitted ✅

  Control-group tests confirm scheduled runs still drive all state effects normally.

- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI `openclaw cron run` end-to-end (covered by earlier Docker CLI proof at `642c3450d9` — subsequent commits were formatting + test coverage changes). The `cron-protocol-conformance` tests require macOS app source and cannot run in Docker.

### Manual-run state contract

`cron run` is a debug/verification command. Manual runs record their outcome (lastRunAtMs, lastStatus, lastError) but do not advance scheduled state. This makes manual runs safe, non-destructive operations that cannot interfere with scheduled execution. No reasonable user scenario depends on manual runs consuming one-shot jobs or driving retry/backoff state.

## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `ccb89e3f11`, 2026-06-02)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `lastRunWasManual` field was missing from the `CronJobStateSchema` and `CronJobStatePatchSchema` in `packages/gateway-protocol/src/schema/cron.ts`. Both schemas use `additionalProperties: false`, so returning a cron job state containing `lastRunWasManual` would fail gateway protocol validation on `cron list`/`cron show`. Added `lastRunWasManual: Type.Optional(Type.Boolean())` to both schemas.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container (`node:24-bookworm`), Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.16.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`, patched OpenClaw at commit `ccb89e3f11`.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On the previous commit (`45efd1abdd`), `CronJobStateSchema` did not include `lastRunWasManual`. Any cron job state object with `lastRunWasManual: true` (set by `timer.ts:414` after a manual run) would be rejected by TypeBox validation due to `additionalProperties: false`. The `CronJobStatePatchSchema` was similarly missing the field, preventing state patches that include it.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
docker run --rm \
  -v "$(pwd):/app" \
  -v /app/node_modules \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=8192" \
  node:24-bookworm \
  bash -c '
    cd /app && npm install 2>&1 | tail -3 && \
    node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
      src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts \
      src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts \
      src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts \
      src/cron/cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts \
      src/cron/cron-protocol-schema.test.ts 2>&1'
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```
 ✓ cron src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (56 tests) 335ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts (15 tests) 54ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts (10 tests) 31ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts (3 tests) 3ms
 ✓ cron src/cron/cron-protocol-schema.test.ts (2 tests) 0ms

 Test Files  5 passed (5)
      Tests  86 passed (86)
   Start at  07:37:28
   Duration  3.44s (transform 2.31s, setup 280ms, import 2.62s, tests 424ms, environment 0ms)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: All 86 tests pass including:
  - `timer.regression.test.ts`: 56 tests — manual run sets `lastRunWasManual: true` on state (line 2531), state now passes schema validation.
  - `service.restart-catchup.test.ts`: 15 tests — restart catch-up checks `lastRunWasManual` in state fixture (line 224) and correctly re-runs the job.
  - `cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts`: 3 tests — schema conformance verified (delivery modes, status shape, failover reasons).
  - `cron-protocol-schema.test.ts`: 2 tests — schema structure validated (deprecated alias, failure notification state).

- **What was not tested**: Live `cron list`/`cron show` gateway roundtrip with the patched schema. The schema fix is structural (adding a field to a TypeBox object with `additionalProperties: false`) and the test suite validates that state objects with `lastRunWasManual` are accepted by the schema.

## Live Gateway CLI roundtrip proof (Docker, HEAD `ccb89e3f11`)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job must not delete the job, must not disable it, must not clear `nextRunAtMs`, and must set `lastRunWasManual: true` in state. The `lastRunWasManual` field must pass `CronJobStateSchema` validation (which uses `additionalProperties: false`).
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container `node:24-bookworm` (linux/arm64, OrbStack), Node.js `v24.16.0`, OpenClaw Gateway built from source at commit `ccb89e3f11`.
- **Steps**:
  1. Built OpenClaw from source on the PR branch
  2. Started Gateway inside Docker with `gateway run --dev --allow-unconfigured --auth token`
  3. Created a `deleteAfterRun` at-job via `cron add`
  4. Manually ran the job via `cron run` (resulted in error due to missing codex-linux-arm64 in Docker — this is the interesting case: manual error on a deleteAfterRun job)
  5. Verified job survival, `lastRunWasManual: true`, `enabled: true`, and `nextRunAtMs` preserved via `cron show` and `cron list`
  6. Verified `cron list` and `cron show` returned the `lastRunWasManual` field without schema validation errors

- **Observed result**: After manual `cron run` (which triggered an error due to missing codex dependency in Docker):
  - Job **survived** — still present in `cron list` and `cron show` ✅
